    <meta name="dc.description" content="We study a mean field game in continuous time over a finite horizon, T, where the state of each agent is binary and where players base their strategic decisions on two, possibly competing, factors: the willingness to align with the majority (conformism) and the aspiration of sticking with the own type (stubbornness). We also consider a quadratic cost related to the rate at which a change in the state happens: changing opinion may be a costly operation. Depending on the parameters of the model, the game may have more than one Nash equilibrium, even though the corresponding N-player game does not. Moreover, it exhibits a very rich phase diagram, where polarized/unpolarized, coherent/incoherent equilibria may coexist, except for T small, where the equilibrium is always unique. We fully describe such phase diagram in closed form and provide a detailed numerical analysis of the N-player counterpart of the mean field game. In this finite dimensional setting, the equilibrium selected by the population of players is always coherent (favoring the subpopulation whose type is aligned with the initial condition), but it does not necessarily minimize the cost functional. Rather, it seems that, among the coherent ones, the equilibrium prevailing is the one that most benefits the underdog subpopulation forced to change opinion."/>

    <script type="application/ld+json">{"mainEntity":{"headline":"Polarization and Coherence in Mean Field Games Driven by Private and Social Utility","description":"We study a mean field game in continuous time over a finite horizon, T, where the state of each agent is binary and where players base their strategic decisions on two, possibly competing, factors: the willingness to align with the majority (conformism) and the aspiration of sticking with the own type (stubbornness). We also consider a quadratic cost related to the rate at which a change in the state happens: changing opinion may be a costly operation. Depending on the parameters of the model, the game may have more than one Nash equilibrium, even though the corresponding N-player game does not. Moreover, it exhibits a very rich phase diagram, where polarized/unpolarized, coherent/incoherent equilibria may coexist, except for T small, where the equilibrium is always unique. We fully describe such phase diagram in closed form and provide a detailed numerical analysis of the N-player counterpart of the mean field game. In this finite dimensional setting, the equilibrium selected by the population of players is always coherent (favoring the subpopulation whose type is aligned with the initial condition), but it does not necessarily minimize the cost functional. Rather, it seems that, among the coherent ones, the equilibrium prevailing is the one that most benefits the underdog subpopulation forced to change                 <div data-article-body="true" data-track-component="article body" class="c-article-body">

                    
                    <section aria-labelledby="Abs1" data-title="Abstract" lang="en"><div class="c-article-section" id="Abs1-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="Abs1">Abstract</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="Abs1-content"><p>We study a mean field game in continuous time over a finite horizon, T, where the state of each agent is binary and where players base their strategic decisions on two, possibly competing, factors: the willingness to align with the majority (conformism) and the aspiration of sticking with the own type (stubbornness). We also consider a quadratic cost related to the rate at which a change in the state happens: changing opinion may be a costly operation. Depending on the parameters of the model, the game may have more than one Nash equilibrium, even though the corresponding N-player game does not. Moreover, it exhibits a very rich phase diagram, where polarized/unpolarized, coherent/incoherent equilibria may coexist, except for T small, where the equilibrium is always unique. We fully describe such phase diagram in closed form and provide a detailed numerical analysis of the <i>N</i>-player counterpart of the mean field game. In this finite dimensional setting, the equilibrium selected by the population of players is always coherent (favoring the subpopulation whose type is aligned with the initial condition), but it does not necessarily minimize the cost functional. Rather, it seems that, among the coherent ones, the equilibrium prevailing is the one that most benefits the <i>underdog</i> subpopulation forced to change opinion.</p></div></div></section>

                                <div class="main-content">
                                    <section data-title="Introduction"><div class="c-article-section" id="Sec1-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="Sec1"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">1 </span>Introduction</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="Sec1-content"><p>In this paper, we analyze a simple continuous-time dynamic multi-agent model and study the limit as the number of agents goes to infinity. We consider a group of <i>N</i> interacting agents (<i>players</i>), who are allowed to control their <i>binary</i> state choosing the probability rate of “flipping” them. This rate is a feedback control that may depend on the state of all players and (measurably) on time. Each player aims at minimizing an individual cost, which is comprised by a <i>running cost</i> and a <i>final reward</i>. We consider a standard quadratic running cost. At some final time <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T>0\)</span>, each player gets a reward given as the sum of two different terms:</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                <li>
                  <p>the first mimics a <i>social driver</i> and favors imitation: the player gets a higher reward if she conforms with the majority, and if the majority becomes polarized (close to consensus); the majority is over the whole population, making the interaction between players of <i>mean field</i> type;</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p>the second models the private (individual) desire to align the state with the sign of a static and predetermined random variable denoting her personal type.</p>
                </li>
              </ul><p>These two terms are possibly competing and represent a classical social dilemma: the former mimics <i>conformism</i>, i.e., the adherence to social norms and is often referred to as social utility; the latter models <i>stubbornness</i>, namely, the aspiration of the agent to stay as close as possible to the prescription of personal traits, hence mimicking a private (or individual) utility. As notion of optimality, we adopt that of <i>Nash equilibrium</i>, and our aim is to understand the system’s behavior in the limit as <span class="mathjax-tex">\(N \rightarrow +\infty \)</span>. This falls into the realm of <i>mean field games</i>, introduced by J.-M. Lasry and P.-L. Lions and, independently, by M. Huang, R.P. Malhamé and P.E. Caines (cf. [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 19" title="Huang, M., Malhamé, R.P., Caines, P.E.: Large population stochastic dynamic games: closed-loop McKean–Vlasov systems and the Nash certainty equivalence principle. Commun. Inf. Syst. 6(3), 221–252 (2006). 
                  https://doi.org/10.4310/CIS.2006.v6.n3.a5
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR19" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e533">19</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 20" title="Lasry, J.M., Lions, P.L.: Mean field games. Jpn. J. Math. 2(1), 229–260 (2007). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11537-007-0657-8
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR20" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e536">20</a>]), as limit models for symmetric many player dynamic games as the number of players tends to infinity; see, for instance, the lecture notes [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 6" title="Cardaliaguet, P.: Notes on mean field games. Université de Paris—Dauphine, Tech. rep. (2013)" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR6" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e539">6</a>] and the two-volume work [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 7" title="Carmona, R., Delarue, F.: Probabilistic theory of mean field games with applications. In: Probability Theory and Stochastic Modelling, vol. 83–84. Springer, Cham (2018). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-58920-6
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR7" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e542">7</a>]. As concerns the finite state mean field games, we refer the reader to [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 14" title="Gomes, D.A., Mohr, J., Souza, R.R.: Discrete time, finite state mean field games. J. de Mathématiques Pures et Appliquées 93(3), 308–328 (2010). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matpur.2009.10.010
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR14" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e545">14</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 15" title="Gomes, D.A., Mohr, J., Souza, R.R.: Continuous time finite state mean field games. Appl. Math. Optim. 68(1), 99–143 (2013). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s00245-013-9202-8
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR15" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e549">15</a>].</p><p>The variable representing the type is introduced in the model as a random field and is treated as an observable static component of player’ state. Therefore, this term introduces random disorder and, to the best of our knowledge, it is one of the first attempts to do it in mean field games.</p><p>In literature, this dilemma has been analyzed from different perspectives: [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 5" title="Brock, W., Durlauf, S.: Discrete choice with social interactions. Rev. Econ. Stud. 68(2), 235–260 (2001). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-937X.00168
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR5" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e558">5</a>] is usually considered as a pioneering study on the trade-off between private and social drivers in (static) binary choice models. In [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 4" title="Blume, L., Durlauf, S.: Equilibrium concepts for social interaction models. Int. Game Theory Rev. 5(03), 193–209 (2003). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1142/S021919890300101X
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR4" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e561">4</a>], a generalization to a dynamic continuous-time setting is proposed, which is not a mean field game, as agents play static games at random times. [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 11" title="Dai Pra, P., Sartori, E., Tolotti, M.: Climb on the bandwagon: consensus and periodicity in a lifetime utility model with strategic interactions. Dyn. Games Appl. 9(4), 1061–1075 (2019). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s13235-019-00299-y
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR11" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e564">11</a>] proposes a model of consensus formation similar to our, where only the social component is present and individual preferences are not considered. [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 2" title="Bauso, D., Pesenti, R., Tolotti, M.: Opinion dynamics and stubbornness via multi-population mean-field games. J. Optim. Theory Appl. 170(1), 266–293 (2016). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10957-016-0874-5
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR2" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e567">2</a>] is one of the rare examples studying the interplay between stubbornness and imitation drivers in the realm of mean field games. However, their mathematical setting is rather different from ours: in that paper state variables are real with Gaussian initial distribution; moreover, their linear quadratic optimization problem is solved by affine controls which preserve Gaussianity; as a consequence, their optimal control is always unique. Models close to the one proposed here, but without individual preferences, have also been introduced as examples of non-uniqueness of equilibria in mean field games (see, e.g., [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 1" title="Bardi, M., Fischer, M.: On non-uniqueness and uniqueness of solutions in finite-horizon mean field games. ESAIM Control Optim. Calc. Var. 25, 44 (2019). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1051/cocv/2018026
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR1" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e570">1</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 3" title="Bayraktar, E., Zhang, X.: On non-uniqueness in mean field games. Proc. Am. Math. Soc. 148(9), 4091–4106 (2020). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1090/proc/15046
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR3" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e574">3</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 10" title="Cecchin, A., Pra, P.D., Fischer, M., Pelino, G.: On the convergence problem in mean field games: a two state model without uniqueness. SIAM J. Control Optim. 57(4), 2443–2466 (2019). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1137/18M1222454
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR10" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e577">10</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 12" title="Delarue, F., Tchuendom, R.F.: Selection of equilibria in a linear quadratic mean-field game. Stoch. Process. Appl. 130(2), 1000–1040 (2020). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.spa.2019.04.005
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR12" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e580">12</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 17" title="Gomes, D.A., Velho, R.M., Wolfram, M.T.: Socio-economic applications of finite state mean field games. In: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A, p. 372: 20130405. Royal Society (2014). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1098/rsta.2013.0405
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR17" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e583">17</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 18" title="Hajek, B., Livesay, M.: On non-unique solutions in mean field games. In: 2019 IEEE 58th Conference on Decision and Control (CDC), pp. 1219–1224. IEEE (2019). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/CDC40024.2019.9029906
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR18" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e586">18</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 21" title="Nutz, M., San Martin, J., Tan, X.: Convergence to the mean field game limit: a case study. Ann. Appl. Probab. 30(1), 259–286 (2020). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1214/19-AAP1501
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR21" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e589">21</a>]). Closed in spirit are also mean field games of interacting rotators [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 8" title="Carmona, R., Graves, C.V.: Jet lag recovery: synchronization of circadian oscillators as a mean field game. Dyn. Games Appl. 10(1), 79–99 (2020). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s13235-019-00315-1
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR8" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e593">8</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 22" title="Yin, H., Mehta, P.G., Meyn, S.P., Shanbhag, U.V.: Synchronization of coupled oscillators is a game. IEEE Trans. Autom. Control 57(4), 920–935 (2012). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/TAC.2011.2168082
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR22" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e596">22</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 23" title="Yin, H., Mehta, P.G., Meyn, S.P., Shanbhag, U.V.: On the efficiency of equilibria in mean-field oscillator games. Dyn. Games Appl. 4(2), 177–207 (2014). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s13235-013-0100-0
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR23" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e599">23</a>], which exhibit a synchronization/incoherence phase transition.</p><p>Similarly to what is contained in the last cited references, the model that we are proposing here has the following remarkable feature: for each <i>N</i>, there is a unique Nash equilibrium for the <i>N</i>-player game; however, the corresponding mean field game may have multiple equilibria. This is reminiscent of a common paradigm in Statistical Physics: finite volume Gibbs states are uniquely defined, but thermodynamic limit may be non-unique, indicating a <i>phase transition</i>. The analogy with models in Statistical Physics can be carried on further: the model that we propose corresponds to the mean field Ising model (or Curie–Weiss one), when there are no private signals/types, and to the random field Curie–Weiss model, when disorder is introduced. The time horizon <i>T</i> plays a role similar to the inverse temperature in the models cited above: the higher <i>T</i>, the smaller the contribution of the running cost. The <i>N</i>-player game as well as its mean field limit are presented in Sect. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Sec2">2</a>, following the general theory developed in [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 10" title="Cecchin, A., Pra, P.D., Fischer, M., Pelino, G.: On the convergence problem in mean field games: a two state model without uniqueness. SIAM J. Control Optim. 57(4), 2443–2466 (2019). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1137/18M1222454
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR10" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e627">10</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 14" title="Gomes, D.A., Mohr, J., Souza, R.R.: Discrete time, finite state mean field games. J. de Mathématiques Pures et Appliquées 93(3), 308–328 (2010). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matpur.2009.10.010
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR14" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e630">14</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 15" title="Gomes, D.A., Mohr, J., Souza, R.R.: Continuous time finite state mean field games. Appl. Math. Optim. 68(1), 99–143 (2013). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s00245-013-9202-8
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR15" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e633">15</a>]. In remarkable analogy with the Curie–Weiss model, we show that the mean field game has a unique equilibrium for small <i>T</i>, whereas several equilibria emerge as <i>T</i> increases. For mean field games with multiple equilibria at least four criteria for the selection of a “preferred” equilibrium have been proposed [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 12" title="Delarue, F., Tchuendom, R.F.: Selection of equilibria in a linear quadratic mean-field game. Stoch. Process. Appl. 130(2), 1000–1040 (2020). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.spa.2019.04.005
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR12" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e643">12</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 18" title="Hajek, B., Livesay, M.: On non-unique solutions in mean field games. In: 2019 IEEE 58th Conference on Decision and Control (CDC), pp. 1219–1224. IEEE (2019). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/CDC40024.2019.9029906
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR18" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e646">18</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 21" title="Nutz, M., San Martin, J., Tan, X.: Convergence to the mean field game limit: a case study. Ann. Appl. Probab. 30(1), 259–286 (2020). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1214/19-AAP1501
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR21" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e649">21</a>]:</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                <li>
                  <p>limit of the unique equilibrium of the <i>N</i>-player game,</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p>minimization of the player cost,</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p>regularization by vanishing <i>common noise</i>,</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p>stability for the best response map.</p>
                </li>
              </ul><p>These criteria are not equivalent, and we are not aware of general results concerning their relations. We stress that selecting one equilibrium does not imply that the remaining equilibria are meaningless. Indeed, the feedback strategy corresponding to <i>any</i> equilibrium of the mean field game is an “approximate” Nash equilibrium for the <i>N</i>-player game, as shown in [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 9" title="Cecchin, A., Fischer, M.: Probabilistic approach to finite state mean field games. Appl. Math. Optim. 81(2), 253–300 (2020). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s00245-018-9488-7
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR9" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e692">9</a>].</p><p>A detailed study of the different equilibria of the mean field limit is proposed in Sect. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Sec7">3</a>. In particular, we will see that a number of different types of equilibria can be identified: polarized/unpolarized (related to the size of the majority), coherent/incoherent (alignment of the final population state with the initial state). In Sect. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Sec8">4</a>, we discuss the selection of the equilibrium obtained by taking the limit of the unique equilibrium of the <i>N</i>-player game. In [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 10" title="Cecchin, A., Pra, P.D., Fischer, M., Pelino, G.: On the convergence problem in mean field games: a two state model without uniqueness. SIAM J. Control Optim. 57(4), 2443–2466 (2019). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1137/18M1222454
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR10" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e708">10</a>], in the absence of individual preferences and with a much simpler phase diagram, this question was rigorously answered, while a rigorous analysis is presently out of reach here. We, therefore, run numerical simulations to capture this selection. We see that there is, indeed, a unique equilibrium emerging from the N-player approximation: it is always coherent, but it could be polarized or not, depending on some parameters of the model. Notably, the prevailing equilibrium is not necessarily the one that minimizes the aggregate cost suffered by the population of interacting agents. Some remarks about the rationale behind the selection of the equilibrium in the case of a finite population are collected in Sect. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Sec10">4.2</a>. Section <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Sec11">5</a> contains some concluding remarks. Appendix contains all technical proofs of the results stated in Sect. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Sec7">3</a>.</p></div></div></section><section data-title="A Continuous-Time Binary Strategic Game"><div class="c-article-section" id="Sec2-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="Sec2"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">2 </span>A Continuous-Time Binary Strategic Game</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="Sec2-content"><p>In this section, we apply the general theory in [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 15" title="Gomes, D.A., Mohr, J., Souza, R.R.: Continuous time finite state mean field games. Appl. Math. Optim. 68(1), 99–143 (2013). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s00245-013-9202-8
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR15" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e729">15</a>] to study the equilibria of the <i>N</i>-player game and of the mean field game in the specific model we propose.</p><h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec3"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">2.1 </span>The <i>N</i>-Player Game</h3><p>We consider <i>N</i> players whose binary state vector is denoted by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{x}:= (x_1, \ldots ,x_N) \in \{-1,1\}^N\)</span>. To each player is also assigned a variable <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y_i \in \{-\epsilon ,\epsilon \}\)</span>, where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon >0\)</span> is a given constant, and we set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{y}:= (y_1,\ldots ,y_N)\)</span>; the components of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{y}\)</span> will be referred to as <i>local fields</i>. The vector state <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{x} = \textbf{x}(t)\)</span> evolves in continuous time, while <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{y}\)</span> is static. Each player is allowed to control her state with a feedback control <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u_i(t,\textbf{x}, \textbf{y})\)</span> which may depend on time, and on the values of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{x}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{y}\)</span>. We assume each <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u_i\)</span>, as function of <i>t</i>, to be nonnegative, measurable and locally integrable. Thus, for a given control <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{u} = (u_1, \ldots , u_N)\)</span>, the state of the system, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{x}(t)\)</span>, evolves as a Markov process, whose law is uniquely determined as the solution of the martingale problem for the time-dependent generator</p><div id="Equ45" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} {\mathcal {L}}_t f(\textbf{x}):= \sum _{i=1}^N u_i(t,\textbf{x}, \textbf{y}) \left[ f(\textbf{x}^i) - f(\textbf{x}) \right] =: \sum _{i=1}^N u_i(t,\textbf{x}, \textbf{y}) \nabla ^i f(\textbf{x}), \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{x}^i\)</span> is the vector state obtained from <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{x}\)</span> by replacing the component <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x_i\)</span> with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(-x_i\)</span>. In order to fully define the dynamics, we prescribe the joint distribution of the initial states <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{x}(0)\)</span> and of the local fields <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{y}\)</span>. For simplicity, we assume all these variables are independent: all <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x_i(0)\)</span> have mean <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 \in [-1,1]\)</span>, whereas all <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y_i\)</span> have mean 0. Each player aims at minimizing her own cost, depending on the controls of all players, which is given by</p><div id="Equ1" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} J_i(\textbf{u}):= {\mathbb {E}}\left[ \frac{1}{2}\int _0^T u_i^2(t, \textbf{x}(t), \textbf{y})\textrm{d}t - x_i(T) (m_N(T) + y_i) \right] , \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (1)
                </div></div><p>where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_N(t):= \frac{1}{N} \sum _{i=1}^N x_i(t)\)</span> is the mean state of the population. Here, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T>0\)</span> is the time horizon of the game. Besides the standard quadratic running cost in the control, two other terms contribute to the cost:</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                  <li>
                    <p>the term <span class="mathjax-tex">\(-x_i(T) m_N(T)\)</span> favors <i>polarization</i>: each agent profits from being aligned with the majority at the final time <i>T</i>;</p>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                    <p>the term <span class="mathjax-tex">\(-x_i(T) \, y_i\)</span> incentivizes each agent to align with her own local field. As <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{y}\)</span> is uniformly distributed on <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\{-\epsilon ,\epsilon \}^N\)</span>, this term inhibits alignment of behaviors, hence polarization.</p>
                  </li>
                </ul><p>From a technical viewpoint, we note that, by rescaling time, one could normalize to 1 the time horizon and multiplying by <i>T</i> the reward. Thus, the time horizon <i>T</i> may be seen as tuning the relevance of the final reward as compared to the “natural inertia” expressed by the running cost.</p><p>Given a control vector <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{u}\)</span> and a measurable and locally integrable function</p><div id="Equ46" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \beta : [0,T] \times \{-1,1\}^N \times \{-\epsilon ,\epsilon \}^N \rightarrow [0,+\infty ), \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>we define the control vector <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\left[ \textbf{u}^i,\beta \right] \)</span> by</p><div id="Equ47" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \left[ \textbf{u}^i,\beta \right] _j = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} u_j &{} \hbox {for } j \ne i \\ \beta &{} \hbox {for } j=i \end{array} \right. . \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar1">Definition 2.1</h3>
                  <p>A control vector <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{u}\)</span> is a <i>Nash equilibrium</i> if for each <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\beta \)</span> as above,</p><div id="Equ48" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} J_i({\textbf {u}}) \le J_i\left( [ {\textbf {u}}^i,\beta ] \right) ,\quad \forall i =1,\ldots ,N. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div>
                <p>Nash equilibria may be obtained via the Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman equation (see for details [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 13" title="Dockner, E., Jergensen, S., Van Long, N., Sorger, G.: Differential Games in Economics and Management Science. Cambridge University Press, Cambridge (2000). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1017/CBO9780511805127
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR13" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e2602">13</a>])</p><div id="Equ2" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \frac{\partial {v_i}}{\partial t}(t,{\textbf {x}}, {\textbf {y}}) + \sum _{j=1}^N a_*(\nabla ^j v_j(t,{\textbf {x}}, {\textbf {y}}) ) \nabla ^j v_i(t,{\textbf {x}}, {\textbf {y}}) + \frac{1}{2} a_*^2( \nabla ^i v_i(t,{\textbf {x}}, {\textbf {y}})) = 0 \\ v_i(T, {\textbf {x}}, {\textbf {y}}) = -x_i(m_N+y_i), \end{array}\right. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (2)
                </div></div><p>where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\nabla ^j v_j (t,\textbf{x}, \textbf{y})=v_j (t,\textbf{x}^j, \textbf{y})-v_j (t,\textbf{x}, \textbf{y})\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_N=\frac{1}{N}\sum _{i=1}^N x_i\)</span>, and</p><div id="Equ49" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} a_*(p):= \arg \min _{a \ge 0} \left[ ap - \frac{1}{2} a^2 \right] = p^-, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p^-\)</span> denoting the negative part of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p \in {\mathbb R}\)</span>. Note that (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ2">2</a>) is a system of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(N\times 2^N\times 2^N\)</span> ordinary differential equations with locally Lipschitz vector field and global solutions. Therefore, it admits a unique solution <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{v}:= (v_1,\ldots ,v_N)\)</span>; moreover, there exists a unique Nash equilibrium <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{u}\)</span> given by</p><div id="Equ50" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} u_i(t,\textbf{x}, \textbf{y}):= a_*( \nabla ^i v_i(t,\textbf{x}, \textbf{y})),\quad i=1,\ldots ,N. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec4"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">2.2 </span>The Mean Field Game</h3><p>The mean field game is the formal limit of the above <i>N</i>-player game, as seen from a representative player. Denote, respectively, by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x \in \{-1,1\}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y\in \{-\epsilon ,\epsilon \}\)</span> the state and the local field of a representative player. Given a (deterministic) <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m\in [-1,1]\)</span>, the player aims at minimizing the cost</p><div id="Equ3" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} J(u):= {\mathbb {E}}\left[ \frac{1}{2} \int _0^T u^2(t,x(t), y)\textrm{d}t - x(T)(m + y) \right] \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (3)
                </div></div><p>under the Markovian dynamics with infinitesimal generator</p><div id="Equ4" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} L^u_t f(x,y):= u(t,x,y) [f(t,-x,y) - f(t,x,y)], \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (4)
                </div></div><p>where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mathbb {E}\,[\,\cdot \,]\)</span> denotes the expectation with respect to the noise of the dynamics and to the distribution of the local field <i>y</i>. As above, admissible controls are measurable and locally integrable functions. Consistently with the <i>N</i>-player game, the initial state <i>x</i>(0) and the local field <i>y</i> are independent, with means <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\)</span> and 0, respectively. As we will see below, the convexity of the running cost implies uniqueness of the optimal control <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u_*^m\)</span>, which actually depends on the choice of <i>m</i>. Denoting by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x^m_*(t,y)\)</span> the evolution of the state for the optimal control, the solution of the mean field game is completed by finding the solutions of the Consistency Equation</p><div id="Equ5" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} m = {\mathbb {E}}\left( x^m_*(T,y)\right) . \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (5)
                </div></div><p>Therefore, we handle the mean field game first by solving, for <i>m</i> fixed, the optimal control problem (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ3">3</a>)–(<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ4">4</a>) via Dynamic Programming. This leads to the Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman equation</p><div id="Equ6" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \frac{\partial V(t,x,y)}{\partial t} + \min _{u \ge 0} \left[ u \nabla V(t,x,y) + \frac{1}{2} u^2 \right] = 0 \\ V(T,x,y) = -x(m+y) \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (6)
                </div></div><p>with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\nabla V(t,x,y):= V(t,-x,y) - V(t,x,y)\)</span>. The optimal feedback control is given by</p><div id="Equ51" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} u_*^m(t,x,y) = \left( \nabla V(t,x,y)\right) ^-. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>Then, we solve (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ5">5</a>) using (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ4">4</a>) to obtain the evolution of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m(t,y):= {\mathbb {E}}_y\left( x^m_*(t,y)\right) \)</span>, where <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathbb {E}}_y\)</span> denotes the expectation conditioned to the local field <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y \in \{-\epsilon ,\epsilon \}\)</span>. It follows that</p><div id="Equ52" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} {\mathbb {E}}\left( x^m_*(t,y)\right) ={\mathbb {E}}({\mathbb {E}}_y(x^m_*(t,y))) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ m(t,\epsilon ) + m(t,-\epsilon ) \right] . \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>By (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ4">4</a>), we obtain the Kolmogorov forward equation</p><div id="Equ7" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}t}m(t,y) = {\mathbb {E}}_y\left[ -2 u_*(t,x^m_*(t,y), y) x^m_*(t,y)\right] . \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (7)
                </div></div><p>Now, we proceed with the explicit solutions of the steps just described. Next, we discuss the solutions of the Consistency Equation (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ5">5</a>) in terms of the three parameters of the model: <i>T</i>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\)</span>.</p><h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec5"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">2.3 </span>Solving the Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman Equation</h3><p>Our aim here is to determine the value function <i>V</i>(<i>t</i>, <i>x</i>, <i>y</i>) which solves (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ6">6</a>). Note that this value function also depends on <i>m</i>. It is convenient to set <span class="mathjax-tex">\( z(t,y):= V(t,-1,y) - V(t,1,y). \)</span> Note also that <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \nabla V(t,x,y) = x z(t,y). \)</span> Using (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ6">6</a>), we can subtract the two equations for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(V(t,-1,y)\)</span> and <i>V</i>(<i>t</i>, 1, <i>y</i>), obtaining a closed equation for <i>z</i>(<i>t</i>, <i>y</i>):</p><div id="Equ8" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \frac{\text {d}}{\text {d}t} z(t,y) = \frac{1}{2} z(t,y) |z(t,y)| \\ z(T,y) = 2(m+y). \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (8)
                </div></div><p>It is a key fact that the equations for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z(t,\epsilon )\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z(t,-\epsilon )\)</span> are decoupled, so they can be solved separately by separation of variables. Indeed, observing that, by uniqueness, the sign of <i>z</i>(<i>t</i>, <i>y</i>) is constant in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t\in [0,T]\)</span>, we can rewrite (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ8">8</a>) as</p><div id="Equ53" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}t} \left( \frac{1}{z(t,y)} \right) = \frac{1}{2} \text {sign}(m+y) \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>that, integrated over [<i>t</i>, <i>T</i>], yields</p><div id="Equ9" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} z(t,y) = \frac{2(m+y)}{|m+y|(T-t) + 1}. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (9)
                </div></div><p>At this point, we can also compute the value function <i>V</i>(<i>t</i>, <i>x</i>, <i>y</i>). Plugging the optimal control in (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ6">6</a>), we get</p><div id="Equ54" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial t} V(t,1,y) = \frac{1}{2} \left( z^-(t,y)\right) ^2 = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} 0 &{} \quad \hbox { if } m+y \ge 0 \\ \left( \frac{2(m+y)}{|m+y|(T-t) + 1} \right) ^2 &{} \quad \hbox { if } m+y<0. \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>Integrating this last identity from <i>t</i> to <i>T</i>, we get <i>V</i>(<i>t</i>, 1, <i>y</i>). Having <i>V</i>(<i>t</i>, 1, <i>y</i>) and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z(t,y) = V(t,-1,y) - V(t,1,y)\)</span>, we can also obtain <span class="mathjax-tex">\(V(t,-1,y)\)</span>. The final result is</p><div id="Equ10" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} V(t,x,y) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} -|m+y| &{}\quad \hbox { if } \text {sign}(m+y) \in \{0,x\} \\ -|m+y| + \frac{2|m+y|}{|m+y|(T-t)+1} &{} \quad \hbox { if } \text {sign}(m+y) = -x. \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (10)
                </div></div><h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec6"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">2.4 </span>Solving the Kolmogorov Forward Equation</h3><p>We begin by observing that</p><div id="Equ55" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} u_*(t,x,y)= & {} \left( \nabla V(t,x,y)\right) ^- = \left( x z(t,y)\right) ^- \\= & {} \frac{1+x}{2} z^-(t,y) + \frac{1-x}{2} z^+(t,x) = \frac{1}{2} |z(t,y)| - \frac{x}{2} z(t,y). \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>Plugging this into (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ7">7</a>), we obtain</p><div id="Equ56" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \frac{\text {d}}{\text {d}t}m(t,y) = - m(t,y) |z(t,y)| + z(t,y) \\ m(0,y) = m_0, \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>where we used the fact that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x^2 = 1\)</span>. Recalling that the sign of <i>z</i>(<i>t</i>, <i>y</i>) is constant and equals <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\rho := \text {sign}(m+y)\)</span>, we can rewrite this last equation as</p><div id="Equ57" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} - \rho \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}t}\log (1-\rho m(t,y)) = z(t,y) = \frac{2(m+y)}{\rho (m+y)(T-t) + 1}, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>which, integrated from 0 to <i>t</i>, yields</p><div id="Equ11" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} m(t,y) = \rho \left[ 1-(1-\rho m_0) \left( \frac{|m+y|(T-t)+1}{|m+y|T+1} \right) ^2 \right] . \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (11)
                </div></div><p>Hence,</p><div id="Equ12" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} {\mathbb {E}}\left( x_*^m(t,y) \right) = \frac{1}{2} \left( m(t,\epsilon ) + m(t,-\epsilon )\right) . \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (12)
                </div></div></div></div></section><section data-title="Equilibria and Phase Diagram"><div class="c-article-section" id="Sec7-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="Sec7"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">3 </span>Equilibria and Phase Diagram</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="Sec7-content"><p>This section is entirely devoted to the analysis of the solutions to the Consistency Equation (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ5">5</a>). As said, <i>m</i> corresponds to a solution of the MFG if and only if it solves such equation. Relying on (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ11">11</a>) and (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ12">12</a>), the Consistency Equation can be rewritten as</p><div id="Equ13" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned}{} & {} \frac{1}{2} \text {sign}(m+\epsilon )\left[ 1-\frac{(1-\text {sign}(m+\epsilon )m_0)}{(|m+\epsilon |T+1)^2}\right] \nonumber \\{} & {} \quad + \frac{1}{2} \text {sign}(m-\epsilon )\left[ 1-\frac{(1-\text {sign}(m-\epsilon )m_0)}{(|m-\epsilon |T+1)^2}\right] = m. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (13)
                </div></div><p>Solutions of (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ13">13</a>) will be called <i>equilibria</i>. We are now going to identify all such equilibria. Moreover, depending on the values of the parameters of the model, we classify them emphasizing the presence or the absence of two different features:</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                <li>
                  <p><i>polarization</i>, which expresses the fact that agent alignment outscores individual preference, namely, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(|m|>\epsilon \)</span>;</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p><i>coherence</i>, which indicates the fact that the majority of the agents aligns with the sign of the initial condition <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\)</span>.</p>
                </li>
              </ul><p>We begin by pointing out a symmetry property: if we denote by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\)</span> the l.h.s. of (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ13">13</a>), then</p><div id="Equ14" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} F(-m,\epsilon ,T,-m_0) = F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0). \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (14)
                </div></div><p>Therefore, without loss of generality, in the remainder of this article we study (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ13">13</a>) in the case <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 \ge 0\)</span>. We can thus specify precisely four classes of equilibria:</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                <li>
                  <p>equilibria <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \in (\epsilon ,1]\)</span> will be called <i>polarized coherent</i>;</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p>equilibria <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \in [-1,-\epsilon ]\)</span> will be called <i>polarized incoherent</i>;</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p>equilibria <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \in (0,\epsilon ]\)</span> will be called <i>unpolarized coherent</i>;</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p>equilibria <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \in [-\epsilon ,0]\)</span> will be called <i>unpolarized incoherent</i>.</p>
                </li>
              </ul><p>Before stating the formal results describing in detail all the solutions to (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ13">13</a>), we provide a visual example, where all the possible situations are depicted. To this aim, in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Fig1">1</a> we plot the full phase diagram in the parameters <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon ,T\)</span>, having fixed <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 = 0.25\)</span>. The right picture is a zoom of the region within the dashed lines. We can identify nine regions, each of them corresponding to a specific typology of solutions to (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ13">13</a>). For example, <i>region 1</i>, characterized by an intermediate value of <i>T</i> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> small, shows the presence of three equilibria, one polarized/coherent, one polarized/incoherent, and one unpolarized/incoherent.</p><div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-1" data-title="Fig. 1"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig1" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 1</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0/figures/1" rel="nofollow"><picture><source type="image/webp" srcset="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs10957-023-02233-0/MediaObjects/10957_2023_2233_Fig1_HTML.png?as=webp"><img aria-describedby="figure-1-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs10957-023-02233-0/MediaObjects/10957_2023_2233_Fig1_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 1" loading="lazy" width="685" height="314"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-1-desc"><p>Full phase diagram for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 = 0.25\)</span>. Different regions identify different properties of the solutions to (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ13">13</a>), both in terms of numerosity and classification</p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0/figures/1" data-track-dest="link:Figure1 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 1" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div><div class="c-article-table" data-test="inline-table" data-container-section="table" id="table-1"><figure><figcaption class="c-article-table__figcaption"><b id="Tab1" data-test="table-caption">Table 1 Total number of equilibria for each region of the phase diagram as in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Fig1">1</a>, and number of equilibria disentangled for their typology (coherent/incoherent; polarized/unpolarized)</b></figcaption><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="table-link" data-track="click" data-track-action="view table" data-track-label="button" rel="nofollow" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0/tables/1" aria-label="Full size table 1"><span>Full size table</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div><p>In Table <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="table anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Tab1">1</a>, we summarize the results in terms of number of equilibria and their typology for all the regions on the phase diagram as depicted in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Fig1">1</a>. We note that in regions 6 and 9, for <i>T</i> small, there is a unique equilibrium which is always coherent, and it is polarized in 6, for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> small, whereas it is unpolarized in 9, for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> large. On the opposite, in zones 2, 3 and 4, for <i>T</i> large, there are five equilibria, and three of them are always coherent. Finally, in zones 1, 5, 7 and 8 (for <i>T</i> intermediate), there are three equilibria. In this situation, we see two different zones: in regions 1 and 5 (for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> small), only one equilibrium is coherent; in regions 7 and 8 (for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> large), they are all coherent.</p><p>Note that the way the number of equilibria depends of the parameters <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> and <i>T</i> is far from obvious. For instance, fixing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \simeq 0.5\)</span>, the number of equilibria is not monotonic in <i>T</i>. Similarly, fixing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T \simeq 3\)</span>, there is no monotonicity in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>.</p><p>In the remainder of this section, we state the results describing the phase diagram, specifying at what times the phase transitions occur. This will also serve to specify the algebraic form of all the curves separating the regions depicted in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Fig1">1</a>. To ease readability, we organize them in four propositions, one for each type of equilibrium of the MFG, i.e., one for each class identified by the possible polarization or coherence of the equilibria, which are listed in Table <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="table anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Tab1">1</a>. As already mentioned, we restrict to the case <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\ge 0\)</span>.</p><p>In Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar2">3.1</a>, we study polarized coherent equilibria <i>m</i>, i.e., those for which <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m>\epsilon \)</span>. We identify four regions for the parameter <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>:</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                <li>
                  <p><i>Small</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \le m_0\)</span>. For every value of <i>T</i>, there is a unique equilibrium.</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p><i>Low intermediate</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 < \epsilon \le \epsilon _*^{(1)}\)</span>. There exists a critical time below which there is no equilibrium, and above which there is a unique equilibrium.</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p><i>High intermediate</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(1)}< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>. There are two critical times, and the number of equilibria varies from zero to two to one as <i>T</i> increases and crosses these critical values.</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p><i>Large </i><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \ge \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>. There is a unique critical time with the number of equilibria going from zero to two as <i>T</i> crosses it.</p>
                </li>
              </ul>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar2">Proposition 3.1</h3>
                <p>(Polarized coherent equilibria: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m > \epsilon \)</span>) </p><ol class="u-list-style-none">
                  <li>
                    <span class="u-custom-list-number">(i)</span>
                    
                      <p>Suppose <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \le m_0\)</span>. Then, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall T>0\)</span>, there is a unique equilibrium <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m = M(T,\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>, [regions 6, 5, 1, 2 in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Fig1">1</a>]. Moreover, <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } M(T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 1. \)</span></p>
                    
                  </li>
                  <li>
                    <span class="u-custom-list-number">(ii)</span>
                    
                      <p>Suppose <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \ge \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>. Then, there exists a unique <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^{(1)}_c= T^{(1)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)>0\)</span> such that the graph of the curve in the plane (<i>z</i>, <i>m</i>) of equation <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z = F(m,\epsilon , T^{(1)}_c, m_0)\)</span> is tangent to the line of equation <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z=0\)</span>. Moreover,</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T < T^{(1)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there is no equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>, [region 9];</p>
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T =T^{(1)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there is a unique equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>, [separatrix of regions 9 and 8];</p>
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T >T^{(1)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there are two equilibria in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>, [regions 8, 4].</p>
                        </li>
                      </ul>
                    
                  </li>
                  <li>
                    <span class="u-custom-list-number">(iii)</span>
                    
                      <p>Suppose <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>. Define </p><div id="Equ15" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} T^*(\epsilon , m_0):= - \frac{1}{2\epsilon } + \frac{1}{2\epsilon } \sqrt{\frac{1-m_0}{1+m_0-2\epsilon }}>0. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (15)
                </div></div><p> Then, there exists a unique <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(1)} \in \left( m_0,\frac{1+m_0}{2}\right) \)</span> such that <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon , m_0),m_0) = 0. \)</span> Moreover, if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 < \epsilon \le \epsilon _*^{(1)}\!,\)</span></p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T \le T^*(\epsilon , m_0)\)</span>, there is no equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>, [region 9];</p>
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T > T^*(\epsilon , m_0)\)</span>, there is a unique equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>, [regions 6, 7, 5, 3, 1, 2].</p>
                        </li>
                      </ul><p> If <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(1)}< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>, there exists a unique <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^{(1)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0) >0\)</span> defined as for the case <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \ge \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>. Furthermore, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^{(1)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0) < T^*(\epsilon , m_0)\)</span>, for each <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\)</span> the map <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \mapsto T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> is continuous, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0) \rightarrow T^*(\epsilon _*^{(1)}, m_0)\)</span> as <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \downarrow \epsilon _*^{(1)}\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_c^{(1)}\)</span> defined here for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \in (\epsilon _*^{(1)},\frac{1+m_0}{2})\)</span> connects continuously at <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon = \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span> with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_c^{(1)}\)</span> defined for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \ge \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>, and</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T<T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there is no equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>, [region 9];</p>
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T=T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there is a unique equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>, [separatrix of 9 and 8];</p>
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)<T<T^*(\epsilon , m_0)\)</span>, there are two equilibria in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>, [regions 8, 4];</p>
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T \ge T^*(\epsilon , m_0)\)</span>, there is a unique equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>, [regions 7, 3].</p>
                        </li>
                      </ul>
                    
                  </li>
                </ol>
              <p>In Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar3">3.2</a>, we study polarized incoherent equilibria <i>m</i>, i.e., those for which <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m<-\epsilon \)</span>: the population polarizes in disagreement with the initial majority. We identify two regions for the parameter <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>:</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                <li>
                  <p><i>Small</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon < \frac{1-m_0}{2}\)</span>. There are two critical times, and the number of equilibria varies from zero to two to one as <i>T</i> increases and crosses these critical values.</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p><i>Large</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \ge \frac{1-m_0}{2}\)</span>. There is a unique critical time with the number of equilibria going from zero to two as <i>T</i> crosses it.</p>
                </li>
              </ul>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar3">Proposition 3.2</h3>
                <p>(Polarized incoherent equilibria: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m < -\epsilon \)</span>) </p><ol class="u-list-style-none">
                  <li>
                    <span class="u-custom-list-number">(i)</span>
                    
                      <p>Suppose <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \ge \frac{1-m_0}{2}\)</span>. Then,</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T < T^{(1)}_c(\epsilon ,-m_0)\)</span>, there is no equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\([-1,-\epsilon )\)</span>, [regions 9, 6, 7, 8];</p>
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T =T^{(1)}_c(\epsilon ,-m_0)\)</span>, there is a unique equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\([-1,-\epsilon )\)</span>, [separatrix of 6, 7, 8 from 5, 3, 4];</p>
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T >T^{(1)}_c(\epsilon ,-m_0))\)</span>, there are two equilibria in <span class="mathjax-tex">\([-1,-\epsilon )\)</span>, [regions 5, 3, 4].</p>
                        </li>
                      </ul>
                    
                  </li>
                  <li>
                    <span class="u-custom-list-number">(ii)</span>
                    
                      <p>Suppose <span class="mathjax-tex">\(0< \epsilon < \frac{1-m_0}{2}\)</span>. Then, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^{(1)}_c(\epsilon ,-m_0) < T^{(1)}_*(\epsilon ,-m_0)\)</span>, and,</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T<T^{(1)}_c(\epsilon ,-m_0)\)</span>, there is no equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\([-1,-\epsilon )\)</span>, [region 6];</p>
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T=T^{(1)}_c(\epsilon ,-m_0)\)</span>, there is a unique equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\([-1,-\epsilon )\)</span>, [separatrix of 6 and 5];</p>
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^{(1)}_c(\epsilon ,-m_0)<T<T^{(1)}_*(\epsilon ,-m_0)\)</span>, there are two equilibria in <span class="mathjax-tex">\([-1,-\epsilon )\)</span>, [region 5];</p>
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T \ge T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0)\)</span>, there is a unique equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\([-1,-\epsilon )\)</span>, [regions 1, 2].</p>
                        </li>
                      </ul>
                    
                  </li>
                </ol>
              <p>In Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar4">3.3</a>, we study unpolarized coherent equilibria <i>m</i>, i.e., those for which <span class="mathjax-tex">\(0 \le m \le \epsilon \)</span>. We identify five regions for the parameter <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>:</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                <li>
                  <p><i>Small</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \le m_0\)</span>. There is a unique critical time with the number of equilibria going from zero to two as <i>T</i> crosses it.</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p><i>Low intermediate</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 < \epsilon \le \epsilon _*^{(2)}\)</span>. There are two critical times, and the number of equilibria varies from one to zero to two as <i>T</i> increases and crosses these critical values.</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p><i>Intermediate </i><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(2)}< \epsilon < \epsilon _*^{(3)}\)</span>. There are three critical times, and the number of equilibria varies from one to two to two to zero to two as <i>T</i> increases and crosses these critical values.</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p><i>High intermediate</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>: <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \epsilon _*^{(3)} \le \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>. There are two equilibria for all values of <i>T</i>.</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p><i>Large</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \ge \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>. There is a unique equilibrium for all values of <i>T</i>.</p>
                </li>
              </ul>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar4">Proposition 3.3</h3>
                <p>(Unpolarized coherent equilibria: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(0\le m\le \epsilon \)</span>) </p><ol class="u-list-style-none">
                  <li>
                    <span class="u-custom-list-number">(i)</span>
                    
                      <p>Suppose <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \le m_0\)</span>. Then, there exists a unique <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^{(2)}_c= T^{(2)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)>0\)</span> such that the graph of the curve in the plane (<i>z</i>, <i>m</i>) of equation <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z = F(m,\epsilon , T^{(2)}_c, m_0)\)</span> is tangent to the line of equation <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z=0\)</span>. Moreover,</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T < T^{(2)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there is no equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon )\)</span>, [regions 6, 5, 1];</p>
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T =T^{(2)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there is a unique equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon )\)</span>, [separatrix of 1 and 2];</p>
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T >T^{(2)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there are two equilibria in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon )\)</span>, [region 2].</p>
                        </li>
                      </ul>
                    
                  </li>
                  <li>
                    <span class="u-custom-list-number">(ii)</span>
                    
                      <p>Suppose <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \ge \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>. Then, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall T>0\)</span>, there is a unique equilibrium <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m = M(T,\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon )\)</span>, [regions 9, 8, 4]. Moreover, <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } M(T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0. \)</span></p>
                    
                  </li>
                  <li>
                    <span class="u-custom-list-number">(iii)</span>
                    
                      <p>Suppose <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span> and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> given by (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ15">15</a>). For <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T \le T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there is a unique solution in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon ]\)</span>, [regions 9, 8, 4]; the solution is <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> if and only if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T = T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>. Consider now the function </p><div id="Equ58" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} V(s,\epsilon ,m_0):= \frac{\sqrt{s}}{8} \left\{ 16 + \sqrt{s} \left[ 16 + 3 m_0\left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon s} \right) ^{1/3} \right] \right\} \\ + \frac{\epsilon }{2} (1+\sqrt{s})^2 \left\{ -2 - 4 \sqrt{s} + s \left[ -2+3 \left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon s} \right) ^{1/3} \right] \right\} . \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p> Then,</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                        <li>
                          <p>the equation for the unknown <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon ,\)</span> <span class="mathjax-tex">\( V\left( \frac{m_0}{4 \epsilon },\epsilon ,m_0\right) = 0 \)</span> has a unique solution <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0) \in \left( m_0, \frac{1+m_0}{2} \right) \)</span> (unless <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 = 0\)</span> for which <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0) = 0\)</span>);</p>
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <p>there is a unique <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0) \in \left( \epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0), \frac{1+m_0}{2} \right) \)</span> such that the curve in the plane (<i>s</i>, <i>z</i>) of equation <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z = V(s,\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0),m_0)\)</span> is tangent to the line of equation <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z=0\)</span>.</p>
                        </li>
                      </ul><p> Moreover, </p><ol class="u-list-style-none">
                        <li>
                          <span class="u-custom-list-number">(a)</span>
                          
                            <p>if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 < \epsilon \le \epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\)</span>, the critical time <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^{(2)}_c= T^{(2)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)>0\)</span> defined in point (i) is well defined, and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0) < T^{(2)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>;</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                              <li>
                                <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0) < T \le T^{(2)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there is no equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon ]\)</span>, [regions 6, 5, 1];</p>
                              </li>
                              <li>
                                <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T =T^{(2)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there is a unique equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon )\)</span>, [separatrix of 1 from 2, 3];</p>
                              </li>
                              <li>
                                <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T> T^{(2)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there are two equilibria in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon )\)</span>, [regions 3, 2];</p>
                              </li>
                            </ul>
                          
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <span class="u-custom-list-number">(b)</span>
                          
                            <p>if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)< \epsilon < \epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^{(2)}_c\)</span> is not well defined. This implies that there are two times <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^{(2)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0) < T^{(3)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> such that the graph of the curve in the plane (<i>z</i>, <i>m</i>) of equation <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z = F(m,\epsilon , T^{(2)}_c, m_0)\)</span> is tangent to the line of equation <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z=0\)</span> and</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                              <li>
                                <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0) < T \le T^{(2)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there are two equilibria in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon ]\)</span>, [region 7 in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Fig1">1</a>];</p>
                              </li>
                              <li>
