                                <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T = T^{(2)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there is a unique equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon )\)</span>, [separatrix of 7 and 6];</p>
                              </li>
                              <li>
                                <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^{(2)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)<T< T^{(3)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there is no equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon )\)</span>, [region 6];</p>
                              </li>
                              <li>
                                <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T = T^{(3)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there is a unique equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon )\)</span>, [separatrix of 6 and 7];</p>
                              </li>
                              <li>
                                <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T > T^{(3)}_c(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, there are two equilibria in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon ])\)</span>, [region 3];</p>
                              </li>
                            </ul>
                          
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <span class="u-custom-list-number">(c)</span>
                          
                            <p>if <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0) \le \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>, there are two equilibria in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon ])\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall T > T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, [regions 7, 3].</p>
                          
                        </li>
                      </ol>
                    
                  </li>
                </ol>
              <p>In Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar5">3.4</a>, we study unpolarized incoherent equilibria <i>m</i>, i.e., those for which <span class="mathjax-tex">\(- \epsilon \le m <0\)</span>. We identify two regions for the parameter <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>:</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                <li>
                  <p><i>Small</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon < \frac{1-m_0}{2}\)</span>. There is a unique critical time with the number of equilibria going from zero to one as <i>T</i> crosses it.</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p><i>Large</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \ge \frac{1-m_0}{2}\)</span>. There is no equilibrium for all values of <i>T</i>.</p>
                </li>
              </ul>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar5">Proposition 3.4</h3>
                <p>(Unpolarized incoherent equilibria: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(-\epsilon \le m < 0\)</span>) </p><ol class="u-list-style-none">
                  <li>
                    <span class="u-custom-list-number">(i)</span>
                    
                      <p>Suppose <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \ge \frac{1- m_0}{2}\)</span>. Then, there is no equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\([-\epsilon ,0)\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall T>0\)</span>, [reg. 9, 6, 7, 8, 5, 3, 4].</p>
                    
                  </li>
                  <li>
                    <span class="u-custom-list-number">(ii)</span>
                    
                      <p>Suppose <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon < \frac{1-m_0}{2}\)</span> and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> be given by (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ15">15</a>). Then,</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T \le T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0)\)</span>, there is no equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((-\epsilon ,0)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m=-\epsilon \)</span> is an equilibrium if and only if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T=T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0)\)</span>, [regions 9, 6, 5, 3];</p>
                        </li>
                        <li>
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T>T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0)\)</span>, there is a unique equilibrium in <span class="mathjax-tex">\([-\epsilon ,0)\)</span>, [regions 1, 2].</p>
                        </li>
                      </ul>
                    
                  </li>
                </ol>
              </div></div></section><section data-title="The N-Player Game: HJB and Numerical Results"><div class="c-article-section" id="Sec8-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="Sec8"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">4 </span>The <i>N</i>-Player Game: HJB and Numerical Results</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="Sec8-content"><p>In this section, we provide a different glance to the problem and consider again the representative agent in a setting where she is best responding to a population of <i>N</i> opponents (note that, in doing this, the population is thus formed by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(N+1\)</span> players). We now derive a new <i>large dimensional</i> HJB equation used to run simulations of a finite population in order to identify the emerging (unique) equilibrium in the finite dimensional model. This approach is inspired by [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 17" title="Gomes, D.A., Velho, R.M., Wolfram, M.T.: Socio-economic applications of finite state mean field games. In: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A, p. 372: 20130405. Royal Society (2014). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1098/rsta.2013.0405
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR17" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e17868">17</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 18" title="Hajek, B., Livesay, M.: On non-unique solutions in mean field games. In: 2019 IEEE 58th Conference on Decision and Control (CDC), pp. 1219–1224. IEEE (2019). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/CDC40024.2019.9029906
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR18" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e17871">18</a>]. Different numerical methods for finite state <i>N</i>-player games are developed in [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 16" title="Gomes, D.A., Saúde, J.: Numerical methods for finite-state mean-field games satisfying a monotonicity condition. Appl. Math. Optim. 83(1), 51–82 (2021). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s00245-018-9510-0
                  
                " href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#ref-CR16" id="ref-link-section-d908650323e17878">16</a>].</p><p>All parameters and variables are as described in the previous sections. Recall that each agent <i>j</i> is characterized by a predetermined <i>local field</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y_j\in \{-\epsilon ,\, \epsilon \}\)</span>, where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \in [0,1]\)</span> and by a time-varying state variable <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x_j(t)\in \{ -1,\, 1\}\)</span>. We take agent <i>i</i> playing the role of the representative agent. Concerning the remaining population of <i>N</i> players, we introduce two summary statistics as the number of “ones” in the two subpopulations with different local fields (i.e., different <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>). To this aim, we define</p><div id="Equ59" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} n^+_N = \sum _{j\ne i} \mathbb I_{\{x_j=1\}} \mathbb I_{\{y_j=\epsilon \}};\quad n^-_N = \sum _{j\ne i} \mathbb I_{\{x_j=1\}} \mathbb I_{\{y_j=-\epsilon \}}; \quad n^{\epsilon }_N = \sum _{j\ne i} \mathbb I_{\{y_j=\epsilon \}}. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>Note that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(n^{\epsilon }_N\)</span> is a static variable, whereas <span class="mathjax-tex">\(n^{+}_N\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(n^-_N\)</span> change in time and take values, respectively, in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\{0,\,1,\ldots ,n_N^{\epsilon }\}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\{0,\,1,\ldots ,N-n_N^{\epsilon }\}\)</span>. By taking advantage of the symmetries of the model, we search equilibrium controls that, for the representative player <i>i</i>, are feedback depending on the state <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x_i\)</span>, on the local field <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y_i\)</span> and on the aggregate variables <span class="mathjax-tex">\(n^+_N, n^-_N\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(n^{\epsilon }_N\)</span>, and symmetrically for all other players. We denote by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha (x_i,y_i,n^+,n^-,n^{\epsilon },t)\)</span> the feedback control strategy of player <i>i</i>, while each other player <span class="mathjax-tex">\(j \ne i\)</span> uses the feedback control</p><div id="Equ60" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned}{} & {} \beta (x_j,\, y_j, \, n^+ -\mathbb I_{\{x_j=1\}} \mathbb I_{\{y_j=\epsilon \}} + \mathbb I_{\{x_i=1\}} \mathbb I_{\{y_i=\epsilon \}}, \, \\{} & {} n^- -\mathbb I_{\{x_j=1\}} \mathbb I_{\{y_j=-\epsilon \}} +\, \mathbb I_{\{x_i=1\}} \mathbb I_{\{y_i=-\epsilon \}}, \, n^{\epsilon } - \mathbb I_{\{y_j=\epsilon \}} + \mathbb I_{\{y_i=\epsilon \}}, \,t), \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>where, for instance, we have used the fact that</p><div id="Equ61" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} n^+ -\mathbb I_{\{x_j=1\}} \mathbb I_{\{y_j=\epsilon \}} + \mathbb I_{\{x_i=1\}} \mathbb I_{\{y_i=\epsilon \}} = \sum _{k\ne j} \mathbb I_{\{x_k=1\}} \mathbb I_{\{y_k=\epsilon \}}. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>Under these assumptions, the triple <span class="mathjax-tex">\((x_i,n^+,n^-)\)</span> is a sufficient statistics, in the sense that its time evolution is Markovian, with transition rates:</p><div id="Equ62" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned} (x,n^+,n^-) \mapsto (-x,n^+,n^-)&\text { with rate } u(t)=\alpha (x,y,n^+,n^-,n^{\epsilon },t);\\ (x,n^+,n^-) \mapsto (x,n^++1,n^-)&\text { with rate }\gamma ^+(x,n^+,n^-,n^{\epsilon },t)\\&=\Big (n^{\epsilon }-n^+\Big )\!\cdot \beta \Big (\!\!-1,+\epsilon ,n^+\!+\mathbb {I}_{\{x=1\}},n^-,n^{\epsilon },t\Big );\\ (x,n^+,n^-) \mapsto (x,n^+-1,n^-)&\text { with rate }\delta ^+(x,n^+,n^-,n^{\epsilon },t)\\&=n^+\cdot \beta \Big (1,+\epsilon ,n^+\!-\mathbb {I}_{\{x=-1\}},n^-,n^{\epsilon },t\Big );\\ (x,n^+,n^-) \mapsto (x,n^+,n^-+1)&\text { with rate }\gamma ^-(x,n^+,n^-,n^{\epsilon },t) \\&=\Big (N-n^{\epsilon }-n^-\Big ) \!\cdot \beta \Big (\!\!-1,-\epsilon ,n^+,n^-\!+\mathbb {I}_{\{x=1\}},n^{\epsilon },t\Big );\\ (x,n^+,n^-) \mapsto (x,n^+,n^--1)&\text { with rate }\delta ^-(x,n^+,n^-,n^{\epsilon },t)\\&=n^-\cdot \beta \Big (1,-\epsilon ,n^+,n^-\!-\mathbb {I}){\{x=-1\}},t\Big ). \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>This allows a considerable reduction in the cardinality of the state space, from <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2^{N+1}\)</span> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(N^2)\)</span>. The best response <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u(t) = \alpha (x(t),y,n^+(t),n^-(t),n^{\epsilon },t)\)</span> for player <i>i</i> is the one minimizing the cost</p><div id="Equ63" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \mathbb E \left[ \int _0^T \frac{u^2(t)}{2} \, \textrm{d}t - x(T)\, (m_{N+1}(T)+y)\right] , \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>with</p><div id="Equ64" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} m_{N+1}(T)=2\frac{(n^+(t)+n^-(t)) + \mathbb {I}_{\{x=1\}}}{N+1}-1. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>By Dynamic Programming, the Value Function for this stochastic optimal control problem solves</p><div id="Equ65" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned}&\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} +\min _{u} \left[ \frac{u^2}{2}+u\nabla _x V +\gamma ^+ \nabla ^+_{\gamma } V + \delta ^+ \nabla ^+_{\delta } V + \gamma ^- \nabla ^-_{\gamma } V + \delta ^- \nabla ^-_{\delta } V \right] =0 \\&V(x,y,n^+,n^-,T) =-x\left( \frac{2(n^++n^- + \mathbb {I}_{\{x=1\} )}}{N+1}-1\right) . \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>The minimization over <i>u</i> leads to the optimal feedback</p><div id="Equ16" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \alpha ^*(x,y,n^+,n^-,t)=\left[ V(-x,y,n^+,n^-,t)-V(x,y,n^+,n^-,t)\right] ^- \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (16)
                </div></div><p>and, finally, to the HJB equation</p><div id="Equ17" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned} \dot{V} =&\ \frac{1}{2} \left( \left[ V(-x,y,n^+,n^-,t)-V(x,y,n^+,n^-,t)\right] ^-\right) ^2 \\&- \gamma ^+(x,n^+,n^-,t)\cdot [ V(x,+\epsilon ,n^++1,n^-,t)-V(x,+\epsilon ,n^+,n^-,t) ] \\&- \delta ^+(x,n^+,n^-,t)\cdot [ V(x,+\epsilon ,n^+-1,n^-,t)-V(x,+\epsilon ,n^+,n^-,t) ] \\&- \gamma ^-(x,n^+,n^-,t)\cdot [ V(x,-\epsilon ,n^+,n^-+1,t)-V(x,-\epsilon ,n^+,n^-,t) ] \\&- \delta ^-(x,n^+,n^-,t)\cdot [ V(x,-\epsilon ,n^+,n^--1,t)-V(x,-\epsilon ,n^+,n^-,t) ]. \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (17)
                </div></div><p>The unique Nash equilibrium of the game is obtained by setting <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha =\beta =\alpha ^*\)</span>, under which the HJB reduces to a system of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(4\,(n^{\epsilon }+1)(N-n^{\epsilon }+1)\)</span> ordinary differential equations in the state variables <i>V</i> and can be solved numerically. Specifically, we use the software Matlab to solve an ODE system backward in time, meaning that the final conditions play the role of initial conditions and the variation is inverted in time (the r.h.s of (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ17">17</a>) is multiplied by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(-1\)</span>).</p><h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec9"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">4.1 </span>Simulations of the <i>N</i>-Player System</h3><p>Having obtained the Nash equilibrium feedback control <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha ^*\)</span> for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(N+1\)</span> players, we rescale the problem to <i>N</i> players and simulate the evolution of the sufficient statistics</p><div id="Equ66" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} n^+(t) = \sum _{i=1}^N \mathbb {I}_{\{x_i = 1\}}\mathbb {I}_{\{y_i = \epsilon \}}, \ \ n^-(t) = \sum _{i=1}^N \mathbb {I}_{\{x_i = 1\}}\mathbb {I}_{\{y_i = -\epsilon \}}, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>which has the Markovian evolution</p><div id="Equ67" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned} (n^+,n^-) \mapsto (n^++1,n^-)&\text { with rate } \Big (n^{\epsilon }-n^+\Big )\cdot \alpha ^*(-1,+\epsilon ,n^+,n^-, n^{\epsilon },t);\\ (n^+,n^-) \mapsto (n^+-1,n^-)&\text { with rate } n^+\cdot \alpha ^*(1,+\epsilon ,n^+-1,n^-, n^{\epsilon },t);\\ (n^+,n^-) \mapsto (n^+,n^-+1)&\text { with rate } \Big (N-n^{\epsilon }-n^-\Big )\cdot \alpha ^*(-1,-\epsilon ,n^+,n^-,n^{\epsilon },t);\\ (n^+,n^-) \mapsto (n^+,n^--1)&\text { with rate } n^-\cdot \alpha ^*(1,-\epsilon ,n^+,n^--1,n^{\epsilon },t). \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>Initializing appropriately <span class="mathjax-tex">\((n^+(0),n^-(0))\)</span>, by independently assigning to each player <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x_i(0)=1\)</span> with probability <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\frac{1\,+\,m_0}{2}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y_i=\epsilon \)</span> with probability <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\frac{1}{2}\)</span>, we run simulations of the above dynamics, eventually obtaining samples for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_N(T) = 2\,\frac{n^+(T)\,+\,n^-(T)}{N}-1\)</span>. Averaging over <i>S</i> independent simulations we estimate the expectation <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mathbb E(m_N(T))\)</span>.</p><p>More in detail, in our first series of experiments we fix <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0=0.25\)</span>, and we take different values of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> and <i>T</i>. Concerning the number of simulations, we set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(S=100\)</span>, whereas the number of agents in the population is <span class="mathjax-tex">\(N=30\)</span>. This figure could appear too small to describe a <i>large population</i>. However, what we see in our simulations is that the expected values of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_N\)</span> are approximating very well the asymptotic equilibrium of the mean field game, except for some <i>transition windows</i> that we will discuss in more detail. Later, in a second series of experiments, we will also consider <span class="mathjax-tex">\(N=60\)</span>. Note that, by increasing <i>N</i>, the numerical problem becomes quickly intractable because of the high dimension of the HJB associated with the <i>N</i>-dimensional system.<sup><a href="#Fn1"><span class="u-visually-hidden">Footnote </span>1</a></sup></p><p>In Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Fig2">2</a>, we compare <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mathbb {E}(m_N(T))\)</span> (red circles, estimated by averaging over the S simulations), the equilibrium emerging in the finite dimensional model, with the mean field equilibria described as the solutions to the Fixed Point Equation (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ13">13</a>) (black lines). Specifically, we consider three different values of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \in \{0.5, \ 0.52,\ 0.6\}\)</span> and we let <i>T</i> vary from <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T=0\)</span> to a large time, where the largest equilibrium value of <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) is approaching the limit value of 1. We expect that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mathbb {E}(m_N(T))\)</span> converges in <i>N</i> towards one of the mean field equilibria solving (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ13">13</a>). This is verified in our simulations; notably, for certain values of the parameter <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>, we see a clear transition from a polarized to a unpolarized equilibrium or vice versa. In fact, by looking at the four panels of Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Fig2">2</a>, if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> is large enough (see panel with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon =0.6\)</span>), the individual behavior prevails for all <i>T</i> and the population sticks with the smallest (unpolarized and coherent) equilibrium. When <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> is small enough (see panel with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon =0.5\)</span>), the selected equilibrium changes continuously in <i>T</i>, as in the large <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> case, but this equilibrium is unpolarized for small <i>T</i> and polarized for larger <i>T</i>. More interesting is the case of intermediate values of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> (see panel with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon =0.52\)</span>). Here, we see a continuous branch of unpolarized and coherent equilibria existing for all <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T>0\)</span>, while a branch of polarized coherent equilibria emerges for <i>T</i> sufficiently large. In this case, the <i>N</i>-player game agrees with the unique unpolarized equilibrium for small <i>T</i>, jumps to the branch of polarized equilibria as it emerges, but for larger <i>T</i> it jumps back to the less polarized equilibrium. We actually see “smooth transitions” rather than “jumps”, but this could be due to the small value of <i>N</i> in simulations.</p><div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-2" data-title="Fig. 2"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig2" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 2</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0/figures/2" rel="nofollow"><picture><source type="image/webp" srcset="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs10957-023-02233-0/MediaObjects/10957_2023_2233_Fig2_HTML.png?as=webp"><img aria-describedby="figure-2-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs10957-023-02233-0/MediaObjects/10957_2023_2233_Fig2_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 2" loading="lazy" width="685" height="538"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-2-desc"><p>Values of <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) (black dots) and <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathbb {E}}(m_N(T))\)</span> (red large circles)</p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0/figures/2" data-track-dest="link:Figure2 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 2" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div><p>Note that this switch from polarized to unpolarized is not seen for all values of the initial condition <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\)</span>, as seen in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Fig3">3</a>.</p><div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-3" data-title="Fig. 3"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig3" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 3</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0/figures/3" rel="nofollow"><picture><source type="image/webp" srcset="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs10957-023-02233-0/MediaObjects/10957_2023_2233_Fig3_HTML.png?as=webp"><img aria-describedby="figure-3-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs10957-023-02233-0/MediaObjects/10957_2023_2233_Fig3_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 3" loading="lazy" width="685" height="569"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-3-desc"><p>Values of <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) (black dots) and <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathbb {E}}(m_N(T))\)</span> (red large circles). Here, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0=0.2\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon =0.48\)</span></p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0/figures/3" data-track-dest="link:Figure3 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 3" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div><p>In the next section, we provide a justification behind the emergence of one selected equilibrium, in case that multiple equilibria are present in the mean field limit.</p><h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec10"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">4.2 </span>A Rationale Behind the Equilibrium Selection</h3><p>In all the numerical experiments, we have performed about <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathbb {E}}(m_N(T))\)</span>, the equilibrium emerging in the <i>N</i>-dimensional system, we can recognize two important properties:</p><p><u>Property 1.</u> The equilibrium <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathbb {E}}(m_N(T))\)</span> is always coherent. Namely, if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0>0\)</span>, then <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_N(T)>0\)</span>.</p><p><u>Property 2.</u> For some values of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathbb {E}}(m_N(T))\)</span> switches from a polarized to an unpolarized equilibrium (or vice versa) depending on the length of the time horizon <i>T</i>.</p><p>Concerning Property 1, when we look at the finite dimensional system, the equilibrium <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathbb {E}}(m_N(T))\)</span> converges, for <i>N</i> large, to one of the values <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) solving (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ13">13</a>). Note that, among those values, there is at least one coherent equilibrium (i.e., an equilibrium with the same sign as <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\)</span>). It is then plausible to presume that the finite population will select one of the coherent equilibria, in that conveying to an incoherent equilibrium would ask for a (implausible) mobilization of the subpopulation ex-ante aligned with the sign of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\)</span>. Property 2., instead, deals with the eventual polarization of the coherent equilibrium selected when playing the finite dimensional game. Here, the discussion is more subtle, since we do not have a clear and trivial explanation of the evident phase transitions we see in the simulations. The simplest explanation could be that the population chooses the equilibrium which minimizes the <i>total cost</i>, <i>J</i>(<i>u</i>), defined in (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ3">3</a>). Interestingly, this functional, being a function of the control, can be rewritten in terms of <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>). We can take advantage of (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ10">10</a>) to see that, depending on the value of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x\in \{-1,+1\}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y\in \{-\epsilon ,+\epsilon \}\)</span>, we can specify the cost needed to reach a certain equilibrium value <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>):</p><div id="Equ18" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} v_{m(T)}(x,y) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} -|m(T)+y| &{}\quad \hbox { if } \text {sign}(m(T)+y) \in \{0,x\} \\ -|m(T)+y| + \frac{2|m(T)+y|}{T\,|m(T)+y|+1} &{} \quad \hbox { if } \text {sign}(m(T)+y) = -x,\end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (18)
                </div></div><p>where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{m(T)}(x,y)=V(0,x,y)\)</span> evaluated at <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m=m(T)\)</span>. This functional describes the total cost sustained by each subpopulation indexed by <i>x</i> and <i>y</i> to reach the equilibrium <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>). We can now derive the costs sustained by the <i>underdog</i> subpopulation (the one whose local filed is opposite in sign to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\)</span>) and by its opponent, namely the one whose local filed is aligned with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\)</span>. With a slight abuse of notation, we denote such quantities with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(-\epsilon )}(m(T))\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(+\epsilon )}(m(T))\)</span> to emphasize the dependence on the prevailing equilibrium. Accordingly, we will also write <i>J</i>(<i>m</i>(<i>T</i>)) as the total cost sustained by the entire system. We have</p><div id="Equ68" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} J^{(-\epsilon )}(m(T))=\frac{1-m_0}{2}\, v_{m(T)}(-1,-\epsilon )+\frac{1+m_0}{2}\, v_{m(T)}(+1,-\epsilon ) \\ J^{(+\epsilon )}(m(T))=\frac{1-m_0}{2}\, v_{m(T)}(-1,+\epsilon )+\frac{1+m_0}{2}\, v_{m(T)}(+1,+\epsilon ) \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>and</p><div id="Equ69" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} J(m(T))=\frac{1}{2} J^{(-\epsilon )}(m(T))+\frac{1}{2} J^{(-\epsilon )}(m(T)). \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>It is not difficult to see that, when considering only coherent equilibria (i.e., <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) such that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\text {sign}(m(T))=\text {sign}(m_0)\)</span>), <i>J</i>(<i>m</i>(<i>T</i>)) decreases in <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>), in the sense that the more polarized the equilibrium is, the lower the cost to reach it. Therefore, we could expect the polarized equilibrium to prevail. However, as said, for certain values of the parameters, this is not the case. In line with our simulations, we now shape a different conjecture. We see that the prevailing equilibrium is the one that, among the coherent ones, minimizes the functional <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(-\epsilon )}\)</span>, namely the cost related to the <i>underdog</i> subpopulation. We rephrase this conjecture in the following fact, which embraces both the previous properties.</p><p><u>Property 3.</u> The equilibrium <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathbb {E}}(m_N(T))\)</span> of the <i>N</i>-dimensional system converges to the coherent solution of (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ13">13</a>) that minimizes <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(-\epsilon )}\)</span>.</p><p>In some sense, abstracting a two-player game played between the favorite player (<span class="mathjax-tex">\(y=+\epsilon \)</span>) and the underdog one (<span class="mathjax-tex">\(y=-\epsilon \)</span>), we can say that the former <i>imposes</i> that the equilibrium will be coherent (and this minimizes her effort), whereas the latter decides about polarization (again, minimizing effort given the previous selection).</p><p>To provide evidence about the goodness of Property 3, in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Fig4">4</a>, we plot the phase diagram of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(-\epsilon )}\)</span> for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0=0.25\)</span> and for the same values of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> and <i>T</i> seen in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Fig2">2</a>. As said before, for each equilibrium value <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) of the mean field limit, we have the corresponding value of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(-\epsilon )}\)</span>.</p><div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-4" data-title="Fig. 4"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig4" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 4</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0/figures/4" rel="nofollow"><picture><source type="image/webp" srcset="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs10957-023-02233-0/MediaObjects/10957_2023_2233_Fig4_HTML.png?as=webp"><img aria-describedby="figure-4-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs10957-023-02233-0/MediaObjects/10957_2023_2233_Fig4_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 4" loading="lazy" width="685" height="834"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-4-desc"><p>Phase diagram for <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) (left panels) and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(-\epsilon )}\)</span> (right panels) for two different values of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>. Here, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon =0.5\)</span> (top panels) and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon =0.52\)</span> (bottom panels). In the left panels, the red points denote the solution <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) corresponding to the minimum value of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(-\epsilon )}\)</span> (depicted in red in the corresponding right panel)</p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0/figures/4" data-track-dest="link:Figure4 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 4" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div><p>Note that, for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon =0.52\)</span>, we see two transitions corresponding to the points where the two branches of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(-\epsilon )}\)</span> related to the polarized and unpolarized coherent equilibria intersect themselves. In the lower panel of Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Fig4">4</a>, we zoom on the right-bottom panel to better recognize such intersection. We see that the two branches of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(-\epsilon )}\)</span> related to the polarized and unpolarized coherent equilibria intersect themselves at <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T\approx 8.9\)</span>. Notably, this point lies in the time interval, where the emerging equilibrium of the <i>N</i>-dimensional system jumps from the polarized equilibrium to the unpolarized one (panel with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon =0.6\)</span> of Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Fig2">2</a>). We do not report all the figures related to the other values of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>, but the same fact still appears, thus corroborating Property 3.</p><p>Finally, we show that the solution <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) related to the prevailing solution <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_N(T)\)</span> does not necessarily minimize to total cost <i>J</i>(<i>m</i>(<i>T</i>)). In Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Fig5">5</a> (left panel), for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon =0.52\)</span>, we plot the value of <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) that minimizes <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(-\epsilon )}\)</span> (bold blue circles) and <i>J</i> (thin black line). It can be seen that the two cases coincide as soon as <i>T</i> exceed the value <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T\approx 8.9\)</span> discussed above. In the right panel of the same figure, we plot two different branches of <i>J</i>, one corresponding to <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) which minimizes <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(-\epsilon )}\)</span>, and the second one related to <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) which minimizes <i>J</i> (thin black line). We see that the two curves differ exactly for <i>T</i> larger than the intersection value depicted above; this is the value of <i>T</i> where we know that the equilibrium <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_N(T)\)</span> in the finite dimensional model jumps from the polarized to the unpolarized one. This shows that the equilibrium emerging in the population dynamics does not always minimize the total cost. In some sense, the unpolarized equilibrium partially favors the underdog subpopulation, in that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(-\epsilon )}\)</span> is minimized among the coherent equilibria.</p><div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-5" data-title="Fig. 5"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig5" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 5</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0/figures/5" rel="nofollow"><picture><source type="image/webp" srcset="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs10957-023-02233-0/MediaObjects/10957_2023_2233_Fig5_HTML.png?as=webp"><img aria-describedby="figure-5-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs10957-023-02233-0/MediaObjects/10957_2023_2233_Fig5_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 5" loading="lazy" width="685" height="272"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-5-desc"><p>Left: The value of <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) minimizing the functional <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(-\epsilon )}\)</span> (bold blue circles) and minimizing <i>J</i> (thin black line). Right: The functional <i>J</i> computed for <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) minimizing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(-\epsilon )}\)</span> (bold circles) and the minimum level of <i>J</i> (thin black line). Here, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon =0.52\)</span></p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0/figures/5" data-track-dest="link:Figure5 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 5" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div><p>We now run a second series of experiment; the aim is still to reinforce the goodness of Property 3, showing that <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathbb {E}}(m_N(T))\)</span> fairly well approximates the coherent mean field game equilibrium minimizing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(J^{(-\epsilon )}\)</span>. For this round of simulations, we increase the number of agents taking <span class="mathjax-tex">\(N=60\)</span>. Concerning the other parameters, we choose different values of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> and <i>T</i>, in order to consider cases where there are one, three or five solutions to (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ13">13</a>). Specifically, in the first panel of simulations, we fix <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T=1\)</span> and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> vary (cf. the first six experiments reported in Table <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="table anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Tab2">2</a>); in the second panel, we fix <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0=0.2\)</span> and let <i>T</i> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> vary (cf. the second six experiments reported in Table <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="table anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Tab2">2</a>). In Table <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="table anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Tab2">2</a>, we report all results. The first three columns summarize the parameters of each experiment. In the fourth, we report the value of <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) which matches what prescribed by Property 3. Columns five and six pertain to the finite dimensional model; we indicate by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\overline{m}_N(T)\)</span> the average value of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_N(T)\)</span> as resulting by running <span class="mathjax-tex">\(S=100\)</span> simulations, with an indication of its standard deviation, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(SD(m_N(T))\)</span>. Finally, in columns seven and eight, we measure the goodness of the approximation by reporting <span class="mathjax-tex">\(|\overline{m}_N(T)-m(T)|\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(|\overline{m}_N(T)-m(T)|/SD(m_N(T))\)</span>, respectively. By looking at these latter columns, it is evident that the equilibrium prevailing in the finite dimensional model aligns with what prescribed by Property 3. This is testified by the fact that the difference between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\overline{m}_N(T)\)</span> and <i>m</i>(<i>T</i>) is close to 0 (the highest value is 0.038 in experiment n.7) and that such difference is always below one standard deviation (the highest ratio is, again, in experiment n.7).</p><div class="c-article-table" data-test="inline-table" data-container-section="table" id="table-2"><figure><figcaption class="c-article-table__figcaption"><b id="Tab2" data-test="table-caption">Table 2 Results of numerical experiments run to check the goodness of Property 3</b></figcaption><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="table-link" data-track="click" data-track-action="view table" data-track-label="button" rel="nofollow" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0/tables/2" aria-label="Full size table 2"><span>Full size table</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div></div></div></section><section data-title="Conclusions"><div class="c-article-section" id="Sec11-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="Sec11"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">5 </span>Conclusions</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="Sec11-content"><p>We have studied a simple mean field game on a time interval [0, <i>T</i>], where players can control their binary state according to a functional made of a quadratic cost and a final reward. This latter depends on two competing drivers: (i) a social component rewarding conformism, namely, being part of the majority (conformism) and (ii) a private signal favoring the coherence of individuals with respect to a personal type (stubbornness). The trade-off between these two factors, associated with the antimonotonicity of the objective functional, leads to a fairly rich phase diagram. Specifically, the presence of multiple Nash Equilibria for the mean field game has been detected; moreover, when looking at the aggregate outcome of the game, several different types of equilibria can emerge in terms of polarization (fraction of conformists) and coherence (sign of the majority at the final time <i>T</i> compared to the sign of the initial condition).</p><p>We have described and characterized the full phase diagram and discussed the role of all the parameters of the model with respect to the aforementioned classification of possible equilibria. We have also analyzed a <i>N</i>-player version of the same mean field game. It is a well-known that in this latter case, the Nash Equilibrium is necessarily unique. It becomes, therefore, interesting to identify which equilibrium is selected by the <i>N</i>-finite population, in case the corresponding mean field game exhibits multiple equilibria. In this respect, we detected phase transitions, in the sense that, depending on one or more parameters of the model, the equilibrium emerging in the finite-dimensional game is always coherent, but it may turn from an unpolarized one to a polarized and vice versa, depending on the length of the time horizon, <i>T</i>. This fact seems to be new in the mean field game literature. At a first glance, we could expect the finite dimensional population to select the equilibrium minimizing the cost associated with the equilibrium for the entire system (interpreted as the collective cost). By contrast, what emerges from our simulations is that the equilibrium prevailing in the finite dimensional game is the one converging, for <i>N</i> large, to the coherent equilibrium that minimizes the cost functional associated with the <i>ex-ante underdog</i> subpopulation, namely, the collection of such players whose private signal opposites the sign of the majority at time zero. Put differently, it seems that the <i>ex-ante</i> favorite subpopulation (namely, the one whose private signal is aligned with the initial condition) imposes the selection of a coherent equilibrium, whereas the <i>ex-ante</i> underdog subpopulation (namely, the one whose private signal opposites the sign of the initial condition) decides about polarization.</p></div></div></section>
