                <a id="corresp-c1" aria-label="email Elena Sartori" href="mailto:esartori@math.unipd.it">Elena Sartori</a>.</p></div></div></section><section data-title="Additional information"><div class="c-article-section" id="additional-information-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="additional-information">Additional information</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="additional-information-content"><p>Communicated by Xiaojun Chen.</p><h3 class="c-article__sub-heading">Publisher's Note</h3><p>Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.</p></div></div></section><section aria-labelledby="appendices"><div class="c-article-section" id="appendices-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="appendices">A Proof of Results</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="appendices-content"><h3 class="c-article__sub-heading u-visually-hidden" id="App1">A Proof of Results</h3><h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec13"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">1.1 </span>Proof of Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar2">3.1</a>: Polarized Coherent Equilibria (<span class="mathjax-tex">\(m > \epsilon \)</span>)</h3><p>For <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m>\epsilon \)</span>, (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ13">13</a>) can be rewritten as</p><div id="Equ19" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0):=1-m - \frac{1-m_0}{\epsilon ^2 T^2} \varphi \left( \frac{1+mT}{\epsilon \, T} \right) = 0, \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (19)
                </div></div><p>where</p><div id="Equ70" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \varphi (y) = \frac{y^2 + 1}{(y^2-1)^2}. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>Before describing the solutions of the equation <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) \)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>, we give some simple facts that will be useful in the proof.</p><p><b>Fact 1</b>. The map <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\)</span> is strictly concave for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \in (\epsilon ,1]\)</span>, for each <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon ,T,m_0\)</span>.</p>
                    <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar6">Proof</h3>
                    <p>This comes immediately from the fact that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\varphi \)</span> is strictly convex in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((1,+\infty )\)</span>. Indeed,</p><div id="Equ20" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \varphi '(y) = - \frac{2y(3+y^2)}{(y^2-1)^3}, \ \ \ \varphi ''(y) = \frac{6(y^4 + 6 y^2 + 1)}{(y^2-1)^4}. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (20)
                </div></div><p>Note also that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\varphi \)</span> is strictly decreasing. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
                  <p>As a consequence of Fact 1, (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ19">19</a>) can have at most two solutions in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>.</p><p><b>Fact 2</b>. The map <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\)</span> is strictly increasing for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T \in [0,+\infty )\)</span>, for each <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m,\epsilon ,m_0\)</span>, with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m > \epsilon \)</span>.</p>
                    <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar7">Proof</h3>
                    <p>To see this, note that</p><div id="Equ71" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial F}{\partial T} (m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \frac{2(1-m_0)\left[ m(1+Tm)^3 - 3 \epsilon ^2 T(1+Tm) - \epsilon ^4 T^3\right] }{\left( (1+Tm)^3 - \epsilon ^2 T^2\right) ^3}, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>which has the same sign as <span class="mathjax-tex">\( h(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0):= m(1+Tm)^3 - 3 \epsilon ^2 T(1+Tm) - \epsilon ^4 T^3. \)</span> Observing that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(h(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \epsilon \)</span>, that</p><div id="Equ72" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial h}{\partial m} (m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = (1+Tm)^3 + 3 Tm(1+Tm)^2 - 3 \epsilon ^2 T^2 \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>is increasing in <i>m</i> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \frac{\partial h}{\partial m} (\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = 4 \epsilon ^3 T^3 + 6 \epsilon ^2 T^2 + 6 \epsilon \, T + 1 >0, \)</span> we conclude that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\frac{\partial F}{\partial T} (m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)>0\)</span> for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \in (\epsilon ,1]\)</span>. This proves Fact2. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
                  <p><b>Fact 3</b>. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall m,\epsilon ,m_0\)</span>, with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m > \epsilon ,\)</span></p><div id="Equ73" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,0^+,m_0):=\lim _{T \downarrow 0} F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = m_0 - m \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>and</p><div id="Equ74" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \lim _{T \uparrow +\infty } F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \frac{1+m_0-2\epsilon }{2}, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>whereas for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \in (\epsilon ,1]\)</span>,</p><div id="Equ75" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \lim _{T \uparrow +\infty } F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = 1-m. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>These are simple asymptotics, and the proof is omitted.</p><p>Now, we can prove Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar2">3.1</a>. We begin with the case <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \le m_0\)</span>. By Fact 3, <span class="mathjax-tex">\( F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,0^+,m_0) = m_0 - \epsilon \ge 0; \)</span> thus, by Fact 2, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0)>0\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall T>0\)</span>. Since, clearly, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(1,\epsilon ,T,m_0)<0\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\)</span> is strictly concave by Fact 1, the uniqueness of the solution to (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ19">19</a>) follows readily and case (i) is proved.</p><p>Now, consider the case <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \ge \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span> (which implies <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon >m_0\)</span>). By Facts 2 and 3, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0)< 0\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall T>0\)</span>. Since <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(m,\epsilon ,0^+,m_0) = m_0 - m\)</span>, by continuity (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ19">19</a>) has no solution in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span> for <i>T</i> small enough. Now, we claim that there exists a unique <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_c^{(1)} = T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)>0\)</span> such that the graph of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z= F(m,\epsilon ,T_c^{(1)},m_0)\)</span> is tangent to the line <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z=0\)</span>. Since <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\)</span> is strictly increasing in <i>T</i> (Fact 2), such <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_c^{(1)}\)</span>, if any, is unique. To prove the existence of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_c^{(1)}\)</span> by continuity, it is enough to show that there are values of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \in (\epsilon ,1]\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T>0\)</span> such that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)>0\)</span>: this is true <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall m \in (\epsilon ,1)\)</span> as <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) \rightarrow 1-m\)</span> for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T \uparrow +\infty \)</span> (Fact 3). The conclusions in case (ii) are now obvious consequences of concavity and <i>T</i>-monotonicity of <i>F</i>.</p><p>Consider, finally, the case <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>. We first investigate the following equation in the unknown <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T>0\)</span>:</p><div id="Equ21" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0)=0, \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (21)
                </div></div><p>that can be rewritten as</p><div id="Equ76" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} 1-\epsilon - \frac{2\epsilon ^2 T^2 + 2 \epsilon \,T + 1}{4 \epsilon ^2 T^2 + 4 \epsilon \,T + 1} (1-m_0) = 0. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>This can be cast as a quadratic equation in <i>T</i>: it has real solutions if and only if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>, and in this case the only positive solution is <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> given in (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ15">15</a>). Note that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon > m_0\)</span> implies that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)>0\)</span>. Now, <i>T</i>-monotonicity and <i>m</i>-concavity of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\)</span> and the fact that</p><div id="Equ22" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = -1+2(1-m_0) \frac{T(\epsilon \,T+1)(4 \epsilon ^2 T^2 + 2 \epsilon \, T + 1)}{2\epsilon \, T + 1} \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (22)
                </div></div><p>is strictly increasing in <i>T</i>, show that there are two alternatives: </p><ol class="u-list-style-none">
                    <li>
                      <span class="u-custom-list-number">(a)</span>
                      
                        <p>if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0) \le 0\)</span>, then,</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                          <li>
                            <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T \le T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, the map <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\)</span> is less than or equal to 0 at <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m = \epsilon \)</span> and it is decreasing: there are no solutions to (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ19">19</a>) in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>;</p>
                          </li>
                          <li>
                            <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T > T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, the map <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\)</span> is strictly positive at <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m = \epsilon \)</span>, concave and negative at <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m=1\)</span>, so (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ19">19</a>) has a unique solution in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>;</p>
                          </li>
                        </ul>
                      
                    </li>
                    <li>
                      <span class="u-custom-list-number">(b)</span>
                      
                        <p>if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0) > 0\)</span>, then by continuity there exists <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_c^{(1)} = T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0) \in (0,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0))\)</span> such that the graph of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z= F(m,\epsilon ,T_c,m_0)\)</span> is tangent to the line <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z=0\)</span>; as above, this <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_c^{(1)}\)</span> is unique. Thus,</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                          <li>
                            <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T<T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ19">19</a>) has no solutions in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>;</p>
                          </li>
                          <li>
                            <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T=T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ19">19</a>) has a unique solution in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>;</p>
                          </li>
                          <li>
                            <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)<T<T^*(\epsilon , m_0)\)</span>, (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ19">19</a>) has two solutions in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>;</p>
                          </li>
                          <li>
                            <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T \ge T^*(\epsilon , m_0)\)</span>, (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ19">19</a>) has a unique solution in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span>.</p>
                          </li>
                        </ul>
                      
                    </li>
                  </ol><p>Note that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_c^{(1)}\)</span> is defined as in case (ii), and by the Implicit Function Theorem it is continuous at <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall \epsilon \)</span> in its domain. To complete the proof of case (iii), we are left to show that there exists <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(1)} \in \left( m_0,\frac{1+m_0}{2}\right) \)</span> such that</p><div id="Equ77" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0) > 0 \ \ \hbox { if and only if } \ \ \epsilon \in \left( \epsilon _*^{(1)},\frac{1+m_0}{2}\right) , \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>so that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> is defined for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \in (\epsilon _*^{(1)},1]\)</span>. Moreover, if this is the case, continuity implies that</p><div id="Equ78" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \lim _{\epsilon \downarrow \epsilon _*^{(1)}} T_c^{(1)}(\epsilon ,m_0)= T^*(\epsilon ^*,m_0). \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>To complete the proof we are left to show the existence of such <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(1)}\)</span>. This is established by proving that the map <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \epsilon \mapsto \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0) \)</span> is strictly increasing, negative at <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon = m_0\)</span> and diverging to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(+\infty \)</span> at <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon = \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>. We use the expressions (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ22">22</a>) and (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ15">15</a>), and the change of variable <span class="mathjax-tex">\( y:= 1 + 2 \epsilon \, T^*(\epsilon ). \)</span> Note that, by (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ15">15</a>),</p><div id="Equ79" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \epsilon \,T^*(\epsilon ) = - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{1-m_0}{1+m_0-2\epsilon }}, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>so <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y = \sqrt{\frac{1-m_0}{1+m_0-2\epsilon }}\)</span>. It is easily seen that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\frac{dy}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } > 0\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y(m_0) = 1\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \lim _{\epsilon \uparrow \frac{1+m_0}{2}} y(\epsilon ) = +\infty . \)</span></p><p>We can write, using (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ22">22</a>),</p><div id="Equ80" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} G(\epsilon ,m_0):= \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0) = -1 + (1-m_0) \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{2 \epsilon y}. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>This shows that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G(m_0,m_0) = -1\)</span> and that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> diverges to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(+\infty \)</span> as <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \uparrow \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>. So the last step is to prove that <i>G</i> is strictly increasing in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span>. We have</p><div id="Equ81" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial G}{\partial \epsilon }(\epsilon ,m_0) = \frac{1-m_0}{2\epsilon } \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}y} \left( \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{y} \right) \frac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } - \frac{1-m_0}{2 \epsilon ^2} \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{y}. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>Using the facts that <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \frac{\textrm{d}y}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } = \frac{y^3}{1-m_0} \hbox { and } \epsilon = \frac{(1+m_0)y^2 - (1-m_0)}{y^2}, \)</span> it follows that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\frac{\partial G}{\partial \epsilon }(\epsilon ,m_0) \)</span> has the same sign as</p><div id="Equ23" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned}{} & {} \frac{\epsilon y^3}{1-m_0} \frac{3 y^4 - 2 y^3 - y^2 + 1}{y^2} - \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{y} \nonumber \\{} & {} \quad = \frac{\frac{1+m_0}{1-m_0} y^2 - 1}{y}(y^2(y-1)(3y+1) + 1) - \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{y} \nonumber \\{} & {} \quad \ge \frac{ y^2 - 1}{y}(y^2(y-1)(3y+1) + 1) - \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{y} \nonumber \\{} & {} \quad = (y^2 - 1)(y-1)(3y^2 +y - 1)> 0, \ \ \forall \, y>1, \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (23)
                </div></div><p>where we have used the fact that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 \ge 0\)</span> and the expression in the second line of (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ23">23</a>) is increasing in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\)</span>. This completes the proof. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p><h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec14"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">1.2 </span>Proof of Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar3">3.2</a>: Polarized Incoherent Equilibria (<span class="mathjax-tex">\(m < -\epsilon \)</span>)</h3><p>The proof repeats some of the arguments seen in the proof of Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar2">3.1</a>. It is convenient to take advantage of the symmetry relation (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ14">14</a>). This implies that we can equivalently find the equilibria in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\epsilon ,1]\)</span> after replacing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\)</span> by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(-m_0\)</span>. For the regime <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \ge \frac{1-m_0}{2}\)</span>, the proof of part (ii) of Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar2">3.1</a> applies with no changes, as the assumption <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 \ge 0\)</span> was not used. For the case <span class="mathjax-tex">\(0< \epsilon < \frac{1-m_0}{2}\)</span>, we can adapt the proof of part (iii) of Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar2">3.1</a>, where the assumption <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 \ge 0\)</span> was only used to prove the existence of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(1)}\)</span>. Here, we obtain the same behavior seen for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(1)}< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span> in Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar2">3.1</a>: to repeat the same argument we need to show that</p><div id="Equ82" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0),-m_0) > 0, \ \ \ \forall \epsilon : \ \ 0< \epsilon < \frac{1-m_0}{2}. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>Indeed, as seen in the proof of Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar2">3.1</a>,</p><div id="Equ83" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0),-m_0) = -1 + (1+m_0) \frac{(y^2-1)(y^2-y+1)}{2 \epsilon y}, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y = \sqrt{\frac{1+m_0}{1-m_0-2\epsilon }}\)</span>. Note that, as <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 \ge 0\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y >1\)</span> for all <span class="mathjax-tex">\( 0< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>. In particular, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y^2-y+1>1\)</span> so that, with a further simple computation we get</p><div id="Equ84" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial }{\partial m} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0),-m_0)> -1+ (1+m_0) \frac{y^2-1}{2 \epsilon y} = -1 + \frac{\epsilon + m_0}{\epsilon }y >0. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>Thus, the proof for the case <span class="mathjax-tex">\(0< \epsilon < \frac{1-m_0}{2}\)</span> can be carried out in the same way as the proof of part (iii) of Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar2">3.1</a> (case <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(1)}< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>). <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p><h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec15"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">1.3 </span>Proof of Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar4">3.3</a>: Unpolarized Coherent Equilibria (<span class="mathjax-tex">\(0\le m\le \epsilon \)</span>)</h3><p>For <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m\in [0,\epsilon ]\)</span>, equation (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ13">13</a>) becomes:</p><div id="Equ24" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0):= \frac{2(\epsilon \,T+1)Tm + m_0 \left[ (\epsilon \, T+1)^2 + m^2 T^2 \right] }{\left( (\epsilon \, T+1)^2 - m^2 T^2\right) ^2} - m = 0. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (24)
                </div></div><p>We begin by observing that</p><div id="Equ25" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \frac{1}{(\epsilon \, T+1)^2} \varphi \left( \frac{T}{1+\epsilon \, T} m, m_0\right) - m, \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (25)
                </div></div><p>where</p><div id="Equ26" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \varphi (z,m_0):= \frac{2z+m_0 (1+z^2)}{(1-z^2)^2}. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (26)
                </div></div><p>In particular, this allows to prove easily that, if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 \ge 0\)</span>, then <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\)</span> is convex. </p><ol class="u-list-style-none">
                    <li>
                      <span class="u-custom-list-number">(i)</span>
                      
                        <p>Note first that <span class="mathjax-tex">\( F(0,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \frac{m_0}{(\epsilon \,T +1)^2} > 0 \)</span></p>
                      
                    </li>
                  </ol><p>(recall that here <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 \ge \epsilon >0\)</span>). Moreover,</p><div id="Equ85" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = \frac{2(\epsilon \,T + 1) \epsilon \,T + m_0 \left[ (\epsilon \,T + 1) + \epsilon ^2 T^2\right] }{\left( (\epsilon \, T + 1)^2 - \epsilon ^2 T^2\right) ^2} - \epsilon = \psi (\epsilon \,T,m_0) - \epsilon , \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>with</p><div id="Equ86" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \psi (y):= \frac{2(y+1)y + m_0 \left[ (y+1)^2 + y^2 \right] }{(2y+1)^2}. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>As <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \psi '(y) = \frac{2(1-m_0)}{(2y+1)^3}, \)</span> we deduce that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\)</span> is strictly increasing in <i>T</i>, except for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0=1\)</span> (in this case it is constant). In all cases, we have that, since <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,0,m_0) = m_0-\epsilon \ge 0\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\( F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,m_0) >0, \)</span> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall T>0\)</span>. Thus, the map <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \mapsto F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\)</span> is strictly positive at the endpoints of the interval <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon )\)</span>. We also have <span class="mathjax-tex">\( F(m,\epsilon ,0,m_0) = m_0 - m >0 \)</span> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall m \in (0,\epsilon )\)</span>. Moreover, it is easily seen that for each <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \in (0,\epsilon )\)</span>,</p><div id="Equ27" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = -m <0. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (27)
                </div></div><p>Then, there must be a time <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> such that for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T < T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) > 0\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall m \in (0,\epsilon )\)</span>, and the graph of the convex function <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y = F(m,\epsilon ,T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0),m_0)\)</span> is tangent to the line <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y=0\)</span>. Set</p><div id="Equ87" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} {\mathcal {T}}:= \left\{ T > 0: \min _{m \in (0,\epsilon )}F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) \le 0 \right\} . \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>The proof of this point (i) is completed as we show that <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}} = [T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0), +\infty )\)</span>. If this is not the case, by continuity, there must be a time <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\hat{T}} \ge T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\hat{m}} \in (0,\epsilon )\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\delta >0\)</span> such that</p><div id="Equ28" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} F({\hat{m}},\epsilon ,{\hat{T}},m_0) = 0, \ \ \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}m}F({\hat{m}},\epsilon ,{\hat{T}},m_0) = 0, \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (28)
                </div></div><p>but <span class="mathjax-tex">\( F({\hat{m}},\epsilon ,t,m_0)>0, \)</span> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall t \in ({\hat{T}}, {\hat{T}}+\delta )\)</span>. To show that this is impossible, it is enough to prove that</p><div id="Equ29" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}T}F({\hat{m}},\epsilon ,{\hat{T}},m_0) <0. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (29)
                </div></div><p>To see this, it is convenient to perform the following change of variables: <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u:= \frac{m}{\epsilon } \in (0,1)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(r:= \epsilon \, T\)</span>, so that</p><div id="Equ30" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0) = G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0):= \frac{1}{(1+r)^2} \varphi \left( \frac{r}{1+r} u, m_0\right) - \epsilon u, \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (30)
                </div></div><p>where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\varphi \)</span> is given in (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ26">26</a>). Note that (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ29">29</a>) is equivalent to</p><div id="Equ31" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}r} G({\hat{u}},\epsilon ,{\hat{r}},m_0) <0, \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (31)
                </div></div><p>where <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\hat{u}}:= \frac{{\hat{m}}}{\epsilon }\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\hat{r}}:= \epsilon {\hat{T}}\)</span>.</p>
                    <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar8">Claim</h3>
                    <p><span class="mathjax-tex">\({\hat{r}} > \frac{1}{2}\)</span>.</p>
                  
                    <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar9">Proof</h3>
                    <p>To see this, note that, being <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \le m_0\)</span>,</p><div id="Equ88" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) \ge H(u,r,m_0):= \frac{1}{(1+r)^2} \varphi \left( \frac{r}{1+r} u, m_0\right) - m_0 u. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>The claim follows if we show that, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall r \le \frac{1}{2}\)</span>,</p><div id="Equ32" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} H(u,r,m_0) > 0, \ \ \forall u \in (0,1). \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (32)
                </div></div><p>Since <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H(u,r,m_0)\)</span> is linear in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\)</span>, it is enough to prove (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ32">32</a>) for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0\in \{0,1\}\)</span>. For <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 = 0\)</span> this is obvious, so we show it for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 = 1\)</span>:</p><div id="Equ89" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} H(u,r,1) = \frac{\left( 1+ \frac{r}{1+r} u \right) ^2}{(1+r)^2 \left( 1- \frac{r^2 u^2}{(1+r)^2} \right) ^2} - u = \frac{(1-u)(r^2 u^2 - (r^2 +2r)u+1)}{(1+r(1-u))^2}, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>which has the same sign as <span class="mathjax-tex">\( p(u):= r^2 u^2 - (r^2 +2r)u+1). \)</span> If <span class="mathjax-tex">\(r \le \frac{1}{2}\)</span> (indeed here <span class="mathjax-tex">\(r<2\)</span> would suffice), <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p'(u) = 2 r^2 u - r^2 - 2r < 0\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall u \in (0,1)\)</span>, so, <span class="mathjax-tex">\( p(u) \ge p(1) = 1-2r \ge 0, \)</span> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall u \in (0,1)\)</span> and for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(r \le \frac{1}{2}\)</span>. This completes the proof of the Claim. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
                  <p>We are now left with the proof of (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ31">31</a>). Note that</p><div id="Equ33" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}r}G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = - \frac{2}{(1+r)^3} \varphi \left( \frac{r}{1+r} u, m_0\right) + \frac{u}{(1+r)^4} \varphi '\left( \frac{r}{1+r} u, m_0\right) , \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (33)
                </div></div><p>where <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \varphi '(z,m_0) = \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}z} \varphi (z,m_0). \)</span> By (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ28">28</a>),</p><div id="Equ90" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} G({\hat{u}},\epsilon ,{\hat{r}},m_0) = 0 \ \Rightarrow \ \varphi \left( \frac{{\hat{r}}}{1+{\hat{r}}} {\hat{u}}, m_0\right) = \epsilon {\hat{u}}, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>and</p><div id="Equ91" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u}G({\hat{u}},\epsilon ,{\hat{r}},m_0) = 0 \ \Rightarrow \ \frac{r}{(1+r)^3} \varphi '\left( \frac{{\hat{r}}}{1+{\hat{r}}} {\hat{u}}, m_0\right) = \epsilon . \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>Inserting these identities in (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ33">33</a>), we obtain</p><div id="Equ92" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}r}G({\hat{u}},\epsilon ,{\hat{r}},m_0) = \frac{\epsilon {\hat{u}}}{1+{\hat{r}}}\left( -2+\frac{1}{{\hat{r}}} \right) <0, \quad \text { as } {\hat{r}} > \frac{1}{2}. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><ol class="u-list-style-none">
                    <li>
                      <span class="u-custom-list-number">(ii)</span>
                      
                        <p>Note that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(0,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = \frac{m_0}{(\epsilon \, T+1)^2} >0\)</span>. Moreover, <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = \frac{1+m_0}{2} - \epsilon \le 0. \)</span> Since, as shown in point (i), the map <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T \mapsto F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) \)</span> is strictly increasing, then <span class="mathjax-tex">\( F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) <0, \)</span> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall T >0\)</span>. Thus, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> has opposite sign at the endpoints of <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon )\)</span>: by convexity there is a unique solution <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m = M(T, \epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0\)</span>. The fact that <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } M(T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0 \)</span> follows from the fact that <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } F(0,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0 \)</span> and that <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \lim _{T \rightarrow +\infty } \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}m} F(0,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = -1. \)</span></p>
                      
                    </li>
                    <li>
                      <span class="u-custom-list-number">(iii)</span>
                      
                        <p>In this case, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(0,T, \epsilon ,m_0) = \frac{m_0}{(\epsilon \, T +1)^2} \ge 0\)</span>. Moreover, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0)< 0 \ \iff \ T < T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),\)</span> as already seen in the proof of Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar2">3.1</a>, point (iii). By convexity of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T \le T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> Eq. (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ24">24</a>) has a unique solution in <span class="mathjax-tex">\([0,\epsilon )\)</span>. The fact that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m=0\)</span> is a solution if and only if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0=0\)</span> is easily verified. Moreover, by (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ27">27</a>), for <i>T</i> sufficiently large <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> attains negative values, it is positive at <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m=0\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m=\epsilon \)</span>, so (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ24">24</a>) has two solutions in <span class="mathjax-tex">\([0,\epsilon )\)</span>. We need, however, a sharper analysis for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T> T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>. Note that, in this case <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(0,T,\epsilon ,m_0) > 0\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(\epsilon ,T,\epsilon ,m_0) >0\)</span>. Thus, by convexity of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ24">24</a>) has zero or two solutions, except for the “special times” <i>T</i> for which the graph of the map <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> is tangent to the horizontal axis. Note that these special times are identified as (<i>T</i>-component of the) solutions in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon ) \times (T^*(\epsilon ,m_0),+\infty )\)</span> of the system </p><div id="Equ34" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0 \\ \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}m}F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0. \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (34)
                </div></div><p> Note that (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ34">34</a>) has no solutions with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T \le T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, so we may look for solutions of (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ34">34</a>) in <span class="mathjax-tex">\((0,\epsilon ) \times (0,+\infty )\)</span>. The remaining part of the proof is based on the following Lemma.</p>
                      
                    </li>
                  </ol>
                    <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar10">Lemma A.1</h3>
                    <p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\)</span> be as in the statement of Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar4">3.3</a>. Then,</p><div id="Equ93" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} m_0< \epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)< \epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0) < \frac{1+m_0}{2}, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>(unless for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0=0\)</span>, where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(0 = \epsilon _*^{(2)}(0)< \epsilon _*^{(3)}(0) < \frac{1}{2}\)</span>) such that </p><ol class="u-list-style-none">
                      <li>
                        <span class="u-custom-list-number">(a)</span>
                        
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0 < \epsilon \le \epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\)</span>, (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ34">34</a>) has a unique solution <span class="mathjax-tex">\(({\tilde{m}}(\epsilon ,m _0), {\tilde{T}}(\epsilon ,m_0))\)</span>;</p>
                        
                      </li>
                      <li>
                        <span class="u-custom-list-number">(b)</span>
                        
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)< \epsilon <\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\)</span>, (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ34">34</a>) has two solutions <span class="mathjax-tex">\(({\hat{m}}(\epsilon ,m _0), T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0))\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(({\tilde{m}}(\epsilon ,m _0), T_c^{(3)}(\epsilon ,m_0))\)</span> with <span class="mathjax-tex">\( T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)< T_c^{(3)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>;</p>
                        
                      </li>
                      <li>
                        <span class="u-custom-list-number">(c)</span>
                        
                          <p>for <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0) \le \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>, (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ34">34</a>) has no solutions.</p>
                        
                      </li>
                    </ol>
                  <p>The proof of this lemma is postponed after the end of this section. The desired result of the solutions of (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ24">24</a>) readily follows from this Lemma. Indeed, in case (a), there is a unique special time <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>. Since, for large <i>T</i>, (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ24">24</a>) has two solutions, necessarily for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0) < T \le T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> we must have <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)>0\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall m \in (0,\epsilon )\)</span>, so (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ24">24</a>) has no solution.</p><p>In case (b), there are two special times <span class="mathjax-tex">\( T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)< T_c^{(3)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>: the only possibility is that, for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^*(\epsilon ,m_0)< T < T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>, the graph of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \mapsto F(m,T,\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> crosses twice the horizontal axis (two solutions for (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ24">24</a>)), for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_c^{(2)}(\epsilon ,m_0)< T<T_c^{(3)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> it stays above the horizontal axis (no solutions for (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ24">24</a>)) and it crosses again twice the horizontal axis for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T > T_c^{(3)}(\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span>. The proof is therefore completed. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p><h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec16"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">1.4 </span>Proof of Lemma <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar10">A.1</a>
                           </h3><p>By the change of variables <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u:= \frac{m}{\epsilon } \in (0,1)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(r:= \epsilon \, T\)</span> as in (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ30">30</a>), we may replace <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(m,\epsilon ,T,m_0)\)</span> by</p><div id="Equ94" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0):= \frac{1}{(1+r+ru)^2} \left[ m_0 + \frac{2(1+m_0)(1+r)ru}{(1+r-ru)^2} \right] - \epsilon u =0, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p><span class="mathjax-tex">\(u \in (0,1)\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(r >0\)</span>, and (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ34">34</a>) is equivalent to</p><div id="Equ35" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0 \\ \frac{\text {d}}{\text {d}u} G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0. \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (35)
                </div></div><p>Letting, as above, <span class="mathjax-tex">\( U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0):= m_0(1+r-ru)^2 + 2(1+m_0)r(1+r)u - \epsilon u\left[ (1+r)^2-r^2 u^2\right] ^2 = 0, \)</span> we have that <span class="mathjax-tex">\( G(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = \frac{1}{\left[ (1+r)^2-r^2 u^2\right] ^2} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0). \)</span> It is immediately seen that (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ35">35</a>) is equivalent to</p><div id="Equ36" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0 \\ \frac{\text {d}}{\text {d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0, \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (36)
                </div></div><p>We use again the identity <span class="mathjax-tex">\( U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = u \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) +\left( m_0 -4\epsilon r^2 u^3\right) \left( (1+r)^2 - u^2 r^2 \right) , \)</span> which implies that (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ36">36</a>) is equivalent to</p><div id="Equ37" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \left( m_0 -4\epsilon r^2 u^3\right) = 0 \\ \frac{\text {d}}{\text {d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0. \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (37)
                </div></div><p>Summing up, the number of pairs <span class="mathjax-tex">\((m,T) \in (0,\epsilon ) \times (0,+\infty )\)</span> solving (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ34">34</a>) equals the number of solutions <i>r</i> to the equation</p><div id="Equ38" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} W(r,\epsilon ,m_0):= \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U\left( \left( \frac{m_0}{4 \epsilon r^2} \right) ^{1/3},\epsilon ,r,m_0\right) = 0 \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (38)
                </div></div><p>such that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\frac{m_0}{4 \epsilon r^2} <1\)</span>, i.e., <span class="mathjax-tex">\(r> \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }}\)</span>. With the help of a symbolic calculator, we obtain</p><div id="Equ95" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} 8W(r,\epsilon ,m_0)= & {} r \left\{ 16 + \left[ 16 + 3m_0 \left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon r^2} \right) ^{1/3} \right] r \right\} \\{} & {} \quad + 4 \epsilon (1+r)^2 \left\{ -2-4r+ \left[ -2 + 3 \left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon r^2} \right) ^{1/3}\right] r^2 \right\} . \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>Now, set <span class="mathjax-tex">\( V(s,\epsilon ,m_0):= W(\sqrt{s},\epsilon ,m_0). \)</span> Thus, we are left with the problem of finding the solutions of</p><div id="Equ39" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) = 0 \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (39)
                </div></div><p>with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s > \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }\)</span>. This last change of variable is a trick to get the following claim.</p>
                    <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar11">Claim</h3>
                    <p>The map <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s \mapsto V(s,\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> is strictly concave in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon },+\infty \right) \)</span>.</p>
                  
                    <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar12">Proof</h3>
                    <p>Using again a symbolic calculator we get, letting <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k:= \left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon }\right) ^{1/3}\)</span>,</p><div id="Equ96" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned}{} & {} -12 s^{3/2} \frac{\textrm{d}^2}{\textrm{d}s^2}V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) \\{} & {} \qquad = 6+m_0 k s^{1/6} + \epsilon \left[ - 12 + 4 k^2 s^{-1/6} + 36 s + 5 k^2 s^{1/3} + 24 s^{3/2} - 8 k^2 s^{5/6} \right] . \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>To prove the claim, we need to show that this last expression is positive. Note that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s > \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon } = \left( \frac{k}{2}\right) ^3\)</span>. Moreover, as <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0< \epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>, then <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \frac{4m_0}{1+m_0}< k^3 < 2. \)</span> Again from <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>, the inequality <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \frac{\textrm{d}^2}{\textrm{d}s^2}V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) <0\)</span> follows if we show that</p><div id="Equ40" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} 6+m_0 k s^{1/6} + \frac{1+m_0}{2} \left[ - 12 + 4 k^2 s^{-1/6} + 36 s + 5 k^2 s^{1/3} + 24 s^{3/2} - 8 k^2 s^{5/6} \right] >0. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (40)
                </div></div><p>Using <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s > \left( \frac{k}{2}\right) ^3\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k^3 > \frac{4m_0}{1+m_0}\)</span>, we have that <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \frac{1+m_0}{2}5 k^2\,s^{1/3}>\frac{1+m_0}{4}5k^3> 5m_0, \)</span> so the l.h.s. of (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ40">40</a>) is bounded from below by</p><div id="Equ41" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} m_0 k s^{1/6} - m_0 + (1+m_0) \left[ 2k^2 s^{-1/6} + 18 s + 12 s^{3/2} - 4 k^2 s^{5/6} \right] . \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (41)
                </div></div><p>Now, for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s \le \frac{1}{4}\)</span> the expression in (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ41">41</a>) is bounded from below by</p><div id="Equ97" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned} - m_0 + (1+m_0) \left[ 2k^2 s^{-1/6} - 4 k^2 s^{5/6} \right]&= -m_0 +\frac{1+m_0}{s^{1/6}} \left( 2k^2 - 4 k^2 s \right) \\ {}&\ge - m_0 +(1+m_0) k^2 s^{-1/6} \\ {}&\ge - m_0 +(1+m_0) \left( \frac{4 m_0}{1+m_0} \right) ^{2/3} \\ {}&\ge - m_0 + (1+m_0) 4^{2/3} \frac{m_0}{1+m_0} >0. \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>For <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s > \frac{1}{4}\)</span>, the expression in (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ41">41</a>) is bounded from below by</p><div id="Equ98" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned} - m_0 + 18 s + 12 s^{3/2} - 4 k^2 s^{5/6}&\ge -m_0 + s^{5/6} \left[ 18 \left( \frac{1}{4}\right) ^{1/6} + 12 \left( \frac{1}{4}\right) ^{2/3} - 4 k^2 \right] \\ {}&\ge -m_0 + s^{5/6} \left[ \left( \frac{1}{4}\right) ^{1/6} + 12 \left( \frac{1}{4}\right) ^{2/3} - 4 \, 2^{2/3} \right] \\ {}&\ge -m_0 + 8s^{5/6} \ge -m_0 + 2 > 0. \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>This proves (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ40">40</a>) and therefore the claim. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
                  <p>Now, using concavity of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(V(s,\epsilon ,m_0)\)</span> and the fact that <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \lim _{s \rightarrow +\infty } V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) = \lim _{r \rightarrow +\infty } W(r,\epsilon ,m_0) = -\infty , \)</span> we have that (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ39">39</a>) has a unique solution whenever <span class="mathjax-tex">\( V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }, \epsilon , m_0\right) >0. \)</span> We get</p><div id="Equ99" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }, \epsilon , m_0\right) = \frac{m_0(1+m_0)}{2\epsilon } + (1+m_0) \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\epsilon }} - \epsilon \left( 1+e \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\epsilon }} \right) , \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>so</p><div id="Equ100" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }, \epsilon , m_0\right) \Big |_{\epsilon = m_0} = \frac{3}{2}(1-m_0) >0, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><div id="Equ42" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }, \epsilon , m_0\right) \Big |_{\epsilon = \frac{(1+m_0)}{2}} = -\frac{1}{2}(1-m_0) < 0 \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (42)
                </div></div><p>and</p><div id="Equ101" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }, \epsilon , m_0\right) = -1- \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\epsilon }}-\frac{m_0(1+m_0)}{2\epsilon ^2} - \frac{ \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\epsilon }}(1+m_0)}{2\epsilon } < 0. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>Therefore, there is a unique <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\)</span>, with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0< \epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0) < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>, such that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(V\left( \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon _*^{(2)}}, \epsilon _*^{(2)}, m_0\right) = 0\)</span>. Moreover, for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m_0< \epsilon < \epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\)</span>, (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ39">39</a>) has a unique solution and, since</p><div id="Equ102" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}s} V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) \Big |_{s = \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }} = \frac{2(1+m_0-2\epsilon ) \left( 1+\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\alpha }}\right) }{\sqrt{\frac{m_0}{\alpha }}} >0, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>it follows that (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ39">39</a>) has two solutions as <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> crosses <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(2)}(m_0)\)</span>. This actually occurs until <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon \)</span> reaches <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\)</span>, where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\)</span> is characterized by the fact that the graph of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s \mapsto V(s,\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0),m_0)\)</span> is tangent to the horizontal axis. This is a consequence of the following monotonicity property:</p><div id="Equ43" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) <0,\quad \forall s \ge \frac{m_0}{4\epsilon }. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (43)
                </div></div><p>This suffices to characterize <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0)\)</span>; to complete the proof, we need to show that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon _*^{(3)}(m_0) < \frac{1+m_0}{2}\)</span>, which is equivalent to</p><div id="Equ44" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} V\left( s,\frac{1+m_0}{2},m_0\right) <0,\quad \forall s \ge \frac{m_0}{2(1+m_0)}. \end{aligned}$$</span></div><div class="c-article-equation__number">
                    (44)
                </div></div><p>We are therefore left to prove (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ43">43</a>) and (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ44">44</a>). We begin with (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ43">43</a>).</p><div id="Equ103" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned}{} & {} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}\epsilon } V(s,\epsilon ,m_0) \\{} & {} \quad =-\frac{1}{8\epsilon } \left[ m_0 \left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon }\right) ^{1/3} s^{2/3} + 4 \epsilon (1+\sqrt{s})^2 \left( 2 + 4 \sqrt{s} + 4 s -\left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon }\right) ^{2/3} s^{1/3}\right) \right] . \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>To show that this expression is negative, we just observe that, being <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\frac{2m_0}{\epsilon } \le 2\)</span>,</p><div id="Equ104" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} 2 + 4 \sqrt{s} + 4 s -\left( \frac{2m_0}{\epsilon }\right) ^{2/3} s^{1/3}> 2 + 4 \sqrt{s} + 4 s -2 s^{1/3}> \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} 2-2s^{1/3} \ge 0 &{} \hbox {for } s \le 1 \\ 4s - 2 s^{1/3}> 0 &{} \hbox {for } s>1. \end{array} \right. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>This establishes (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ43">43</a>). Now, we show (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ44">44</a>). We recall that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s \mapsto V\left( s,\frac{1+m_0}{2},m_0\right) \)</span> is strictly concave for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s \ge \frac{m_0}{2(1+m_0)}\)</span>. Moreover, again with the help of a symbolic calculator</p><div id="Equ105" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}s} V\left( s,\frac{1+m_0}{2},m_0\right) \Big |_{s = \frac{m_0}{2(1+m_0)}} = 0. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>So it is enough to show that</p><div id="Equ106" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} V\left( \frac{m_0}{2(1+m_0)},\frac{1+m_0}{2},m_0\right) <0, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>that has been seen already in (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ42">42</a>). The proof is now complete. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p><h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec17"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">1.5 </span>Proof of Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar5">3.4</a>: Unpolarized Incoherent Equilibria (<span class="mathjax-tex">\(-\epsilon \le m < 0\)</span>)</h3><p>As done in Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar3">3.2</a>, we use the symmetry (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ14">14</a>). So we look for solutions <span class="mathjax-tex">\(m \in (0,\epsilon ]\)</span> of the equation <span class="mathjax-tex">\( F(m,\epsilon ,T,-m_0) = 0; \)</span> this allows to reuse some of the ideas in Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar4">3.3</a>. </p><ol class="u-list-style-none">
                    <li>
                      <span class="u-custom-list-number">(i)</span>
                      
                        <p>We employ here the change of variables seen in (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ30">30</a>), so </p><div id="Equ107" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned} F(m,\epsilon ,T,-m_0)&= G(u,\epsilon ,r,-m_0):= \frac{1}{(1+r)^2} \varphi \left( \frac{r}{1+r} u, -m_0\right) - \epsilon u \\ {}&= \frac{2(1+r)ru-m_0\left[ (1+r)^2+r^2 u^2\right] }{\left( (1+r)^2-r^2 u^2\right) ^2} - \epsilon u \\&= \frac{1}{(1+r+ru)^2} \left[ -m_0 + \frac{2(1-m_0)(1+r)ru}{(1+r-ru)^2} \right] - \epsilon u. \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p> We need to show that this last expression is strictly negative <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall u\in (0,1)\)</span>; it is enough to show this for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\epsilon = \frac{1-m_0}{2}\)</span>. This amounts to prove that </p><div id="Equ108" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} -m_0 + \frac{2(1-m_0)(1+r)ru}{(1+r-ru)^2} < \frac{1-m_0}{2} u (1+r+ru)^2, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p> which follows from </p><div id="Equ109" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned}{} & {} \frac{2(1-m_0)(1+r)ru}{(1+r-ru)^2} \\{} & {} \quad< \frac{1-m_0}{2} u (1+r+ru)^2 \\{} & {} \qquad \iff \ 4r(1+r) < \left[ (1+r)^2 - r^2 u^2 \right] ^2, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall u \in (0,1)\)</span>. This last inequality follows if we show it holds for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u=1\)</span>, i.e., <span class="mathjax-tex">\( 4r(1+r) < (1+2r)^2, \)</span> that is clearly true <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\forall r>0\)</span>.</p>
                      
                    </li>
                    <li>
                      <span class="u-custom-list-number">(ii)</span>
                      
                        <p>As seen in the proof of point (i) of Proposition <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#FPar4">3.3</a>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,-m_0)\)</span> is strictly increasing in <i>T</i>. Moreover, the same computation done after (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ21">21</a>) shows that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(F(\epsilon ,\epsilon ,T,-m_0)>0\)</span> if and only if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T>T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0)\)</span>. By the same change of variables used in point (i), (<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="equation anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s10957-023-02233-0#Equ24">24</a>) is equivalent to the equation </p><div id="Equ110" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{(1+r+ru)^2} \left[ -m_0 + \frac{2(1-m_0)(1+r)ru}{(1+r-ru)^2} \right] - \epsilon u =0, \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p> with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u \in (0,1)\)</span>, which is also equivalent to </p><div id="Equ111" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned}{} & {} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0):= -m_0(1+r-ru)^2 \\{} & {} \qquad + 2(1-m_0)r(1+r)u - \epsilon u\left[ (1+r)^2-r^2 u^2\right] ^2 = 0. \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p> Our proof is based on the following claim.</p>
                      
                    </li>
                  </ol>
                    <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar13">Claim</h3>
                    <p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u^* \in (0,1)\)</span> be such that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(U(u^*,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = 0\)</span>. Then, <span class="mathjax-tex">\( \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u^*,\epsilon ,r,m_0)>0. \)</span></p>
                  
                    <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar14">Proof</h3>
                    <p>We use the identity</p><div id="Equ112" class="c-article-equation"><div class="c-article-equation__content"><span class="mathjax-tex">$$\begin{aligned} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0)= & {} u \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) +\left( -m_0 -4\epsilon r^2 u^3\right) \left( (1+r)^2 - u^2 r^2 \right) \\< & {} u \frac{\textrm{d}}{\textrm{d}u} U(u,\epsilon ,r,m_0) \end{aligned}$$</span></div></div><p>which proves the claim. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
                  <p>This clearly implies that such <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u^*\)</span> exists if and only if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(U(1,\epsilon ,r,m_0) >0\)</span> (as <span class="mathjax-tex">\(U(0,\epsilon ,r,m_0) = -m_0(1+r)^2\le 0\)</span>), i.e., if and only if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(r > \epsilon \, T^*(\epsilon ,-m_0)\)</span> and in this case it is unique. This completes the proof. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p></div></div></section><section data-title="Rights and permissions"><div class="c-article-section" id="rightslink-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="rightslink">Rights and permissions</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="rightslink-content">
